text: I found Katie to be extremely passive-aggressive and relatively cocky at times. Sometimes she acts like she knows all the material but cannot answer simple questions about it. For SI110, if you have the option to take a discussion with a different instructor, I would recommend that.
She tends to be very passive aggressive during discussions. She seems to know what she is teaching but I don't really appreciate her attitude towards her students.

The professor is awful. Escape from her if you want a fair grading: she will grade assignments with the standards that she never mentioned in the rubric. It's the first time I've met this bad teaching in this University. Run.

She is very rude to students and the assignments have contradicting rubrics. Participation is tracked by raising your hand and saying your name in class, often missing students in the 150+ person lecture room. Writing assignments are essentially the same every time and have unrealistic expectations. This school's faculty has fallen off...

Prof has a lot of unrealistic expectations and is kind of rude. There are 4 major paper assignments where you essentially write about the same thing over and over (with extremely picky grading). There's also a lot of busy work and lectures are unenjoyable. There are better ULWR courses than this one, so save your time and skip this class.
Inadequate guidelines for AI usage and outward disrespect for students. She has been verbally demeaning to students on many occasions during lecture.
Unrealistic expectation for AI use considering it is a School of Information course. Picky grading based word for word, would not recommend.

Professor Toyama is a magical educator! He maximizes students' acquisition in a natural and engaging way. I have 2 Master's in Education and 8 years' work experiences in teaching and curriculum development. I had my once-of-a-life-time experience from his course in which the skills he taught just within one semester turns out to be my strong suit.

Kentaro's respect for the material he handles as well as his students is materially apparent, and I cannot underscore enough that even if his class was at 3:30 a.m. (it was 8 when I took it), it's worth it.

Chillest class for my first semester of grad school. I loved this class and Kentaro. That said, I was lucky to have a good group. Class is very very heavy on group projects. Experience may differ base on group dynamics, but you can always ask to be switch.
Kentaro is an awesome scholar. He cares a lot about education and the practical ways to conduct research and experimentation. If you ever get a chance to work with him, do it!

I took Contextual Inquiry with Professor Toyama and he was an incredible instructor who provided clear instruction and guidance for our semester-long group project! He is one of those professors who encourages his students to learn in the class and outside of class. Definitely have learned many things from him / his office hours! Amazing Instructor

Professor Kentaro Toyama is an exceptional person with the passion and energy more than double of many of his own students. He respects every student and is always down to earth irrespective of his own reputation. He is kind and approachable and puts tremendous effort into planning his courses. He keeps all of his students engaged and productive.

Kentaro's course is well structured and the expectations are reasonable. I gained a lot of practical experience in the course's subject matter, which was crucial to learning the methodologies we covered in the course. Kentaro was very knowledgeable, engaging, and provided great feedback.

I liked that course feedback was solicited throughout the semester--that is the most beneficial for students currently enrolled. I also enjoyed that we could offer feedback for our team members throughout the course in a formalized method. Kentaro is an engaging lecturer. Learning to interact with groups. I hate group based work but ultimately the

Dr. Gray was great in terms of how she presented the content and it may have been her first time teaching this class, but the class itself was not well-structured. Almost all of our discussion meetings got cancelled and I hoped to get more from the class, but this class ended up being one of the least to worry about in terms of workload.

There is a lot I love about dr. gray. She's funny, energetic, and really gets you thinking about so much going on in the world. That being said, this class was quite unorganized where there many meetings got canceled. Still I enjoyed the content I just wish I could participate in discussion more. Assigments are the same question 5 times.

Although the course includes many assignments and full completion isn't required, your final grade depends on the total credits accumulated. Since grading can be inconsistent and some graders are stricter than others, it's best to finish as many assignments as you can to aim for an A. That said, many of the assignments tend to feel repetitive.

Prof. Laurie is truly exceptional and stands out as the finest professor I've had the privilege to learn from. Despite my personal challenges, she supported me through graduation, with engaging, insightful assignments. Her adaptability, compassion, and dedication create an ideal learning environment, making every effort count.

Prof. Buis uses a gamified approach, meaning there are ~11,000,000 assignments. You don't have to do them, but since you start with 0 points, it feels like you do. That aside, she's really willing to discuss/be flexible about certain grading metrics if people feel something wasn't clear. I don't love the class, but it's not bad; she's very fair.

Laurie claims that the vast amount of homework serves to reinforce our learning. In reality, it is just such a nuisance to repeatedly trying to milk the same knowledge for slightly different contexts. This is effort for nothing, I'm not learning anything new or better.

Lija is a really passionate professor and it shows. She really cares about how much you learn and will give you a lot of feedback to ensure that you're learning a lot. Overall, I learned a lot of really useful research methods and ideas. The only cons were that the lectures can be a bit boring sometimes, but I think the assignments make up for it.

Great professor! She is very enthusiastic about the usability field and I would highly recommend taking advantage of that. The lectures can get a little dry at parts, but it's a great class. Unfortunately my group was bad so that made the class more difficult, but I still enjoyed it!

I loved this class. Having sat in the front and talked to Lija more, the class was interesting and I was able to use these skills on outside projects. She also offered advice for internships and the field in general.

Lija brings a lot of great industry experience to usability - a great prof too! The class is pretty good, even if the lectures aren't overly interesting. Make sure you get a good group because there are very few individual assignments. With a good group the class goes very smoothly.

I didn't personally connect with the subject matter, but he explained content with good examples that helped cement ideas. The exams were straightforward & he was understanding to student needs. He mumbled a bit in class which made it hard to hear, but overall he was a decent teacher. Wouldn't strive to take again, but wouldn't be upset if I did.
cares about students, will get an A if you just put in effort

310 is a class that every BSI junior dreads, but he did a good job at making it tolerable. This was his first semester teaching the class and the curriculum wasn't what he wanted it to be since he just used the previous instructor's curriculum. I know in 2020 he will mold this class more in his image. Pray you win the GSI lottery.

Lectures are technically mandatory, but your grade won't suffer if you miss a few. Lectures are mostly tangents and you can get by just reading the slides. The cases are complicated and ambiguous, and you can never tell how well you will do. The exams are a lot better and more clear. Very unclear what final grades will look like.

Professor Robert and his instruction team are great. SI588 included very interesting topics in human-computer interaction. Do well in the three super interesting and creative take-home exams and 2 reflection papers+12 in-class mini exercise and you get an A. This class is really fun, recommended.

Doing the readings was not entirely necessary as it was all covered in lectures. A lot of work for this class, but you learn a lot as well. Just do the assignments as requested and you get a decent grade

Nice guy, very good lectures that were easy to follow along. Sometimes the project specs werent very clear but the staff would always answer questions. Did bring up some of the same few projects in different lectures, but he is passionate and he does care.

Written for Winter 2024-25 semester for EECS 493 Actually shocked to see Professor Ackerman have such a bad score on RMP. He's genuinely a great dude. Don't understand the hate. If you hate how EECS 493 is, hate the class and the many lecturers, not just Ackerman. Easy class. Ackerman makes the dead easy lectures better by being cool.
quiz and quiz answers make no sense. This professor should have lower rating than Edward Ionides.

Prof. Ackerman and his EECS 496 class was honestly one of the worst classes I've ever taken. His syllabus and grading criteria are unclear. His assignments have unclear rubrics and he gives out arbitrary grades.
Not clear grading criteria, difficult to assess expectations

He's not a great lecturer, but as a professor, he was okay in terms of class administration. We often had class administration complains because of the other professor of this class, and Professor Ackerman would usually help to fix the issue.
Absolutely horrible communicator, has no idea where students' understanding is, WORST EECS professor in the department WITHOUT QUESTION
You don't learn anything on EECS 493 and the grade is so bad. I think he is the worst professor I have ever seen. Do not take his class unless you want to waste your time.

Ackermans User Interface course is comparable to (Insert your least favorite LSA course here). If you hope to learn about programmatically building user interfaces, or about current design trends, then this is not the course for you. If you like memorizing flash cards, and regurgitating useless information to do well on exams, then take UI.

The course itself isn't too interesting but easy to understand. He tries really hard to make the lecture more fun. Assignments are done using the Qt framework, which isn't explained in class, so you need to do a lot of your own research. Although, grading isn't too tough.

I really couldn't say what exactly it is about his lecture style that makes it so boring, but it is. I learned a lot about UI during the class- from watching YouTube videos so I could manage the homework. Lectures are pretty scattered and don't have a clear path, and he tends to assume you have prior knowledge about UI programming, web design, etc.
Mark was pretty funny and lectures were entertaining, but not educational. I felt like I learned nothing and lectures helped not at all with exams.

It's teachers like Mark Akerman that make you want to drop out of school. He acknowledges that he's the most boring lecturer and doesn't do anything about it. Was really interested in the class, professor completely ruined it. I have a 3.5 but might fail this class. Couldn't really care less anymore.

I was really looking forward to this course, and it was fine for the first half. I didn't learn a single thing the second half of the course. In fact, the review sheet for the final was the same as the review sheet for the midterm plus two or three additional things. Waste of my time.
great course and interesting man
great course!
funny man and interesting course!
Great! Excellent! Hard work but so wroth it.
Great teacher! Fun class, fun guy. Truly enjoyed the course, but was very challenged.
brilliant guy, good sense of humor. wish he responded to email.
He is the Best!!!!!!!!!! and I mean it
Very entertaining teacher. Makes lectures interesting. One of the best EECS professors, hands down.
Very good lecturer, always kept things interesting. Stories and chalkboard more than just Power-Point.
I would have liked to know more about new kinds of interfaces. He has a good sense of humor and seemed to genuinely like us.
Good about answering questions and fielding students' concerns. Great availability.
Overall, pretty good - lectures somewhat lengthy but covered the important information. Tough - but what can you say, it's EECS.
Has read world experience in subject matter, not experience in teaching.

Dont even think about it... completely incompetent teacher and useless for all intents and purposes. Assignments are hard and given in language more cryptic than the incomprehensible examples he gives in class.... enjoy!

Took COMPFOR 221 w Prof G. He's kind and definitely cares about his students and his class. My bigger issues where with the class structure, which moved way too fast for an intro python course. I was concerned I wouldn't pass bc the quizzes were very difficult as a beginner. He can make you feel dumb if you're not CS, but I don't think he means to.

*I took Prof G for COMPFOR 221* Professor G is nice, but probably shouldn't teach non-majors. Even CS majors told me he moved really fast during our class (which never even reviewed python basics). When coming to him with problems he wasn't always patient and interrupted me on multiple occasions. Sometimes he even laughed at my questions/mistakes.

Arrogant and refuses to admit he could ever make a human mistake, even when a student asks for clarification on contradicting material being taught. Officially on my list of professors to avoid at all costs.

The whole course is disorder. IAs did not know how to solve practice problem, and the instructors do not provide the answer of practice problems. Many questions in final exam do not make sense, but they make the piazza inactive.
Questions on anything!!! Ope, except for that. Nope, you're wrong, only my answer is right. What do you mean, I said any of these answers would be correct last lecture?
He has high enthusiasm. Everything else he is just awful.

I was one of those who gave Mark a low rate at the beginning of this semester due to the miscommunication fight on the piazza. However, I totally changed my mind at the end of the semester. When he said that the teaching team lacks resources, it's true due to some IAs/graders. Mark is open-minded and always prepare for his lecture very well.

It is really horrible course. The material and content are easy, but the professor do not know how to teach the course and assume every student knows everything. He does not care about whether students really learn something. Many students compliant, but he just ignored. If I have choice again, I will definitely not take the course.

Horrible professor. Speaks too quickly, brushes over many topics, goes off on tangents in the middle of sentences which detracts from the main idea, and lecture slides frequently consist of a title and a picture, which make them unusable for studying. Extremely disorganized. Information on course logistics is scattered in multiple different places

He's a good Teacher and a lecturer, but he is not a good professor for class administration. There were often complaints and arguments in the Piazza about unfair weekly quiz questions. I also had the impression of him being a little bit obsessed with "teamwork" too, demanding MANY things to be team based unnecessarily and inconveniently.

Chill teacher that is unassuming and energetic. Very knowledgable in educational methods, so his lectures are engaging, dynamic, and often fun. He specializes in computer science education research, so he brings a unique perspective compared to most other profs. If you're interested in computer science and education, highly recommend!

Mark was great! I didn't think I'd learn much from this class after already taking my MDE and 485, but I did learn quite a bit about UI! Class is really easy, extra credit on assignments, but make sure you don't forget about the weekly quizzes.
Mark really cares about his students. Very enthusiastic too

The professor was condescending and dismissive, consistently showing a lack of respect for students' opinions. Their feedback was unconstructive, often belittling rather than guiding. They seemed more interested in asserting authority than fostering a positive learning environment. Overall, a very unprofessional and unpleasant experience.

Avoid this professor for SI206. Terribly organized, completely overloads deadlines by making everything due at the same time. Awful lectures where you simply copy and paste code without getting any conceptual understanding of what is going on, which is a shame bc the course material itself is quite interesting.
Try to avoid this professor at all costs. His lectures and the formatting of the class is a joke.

ok so easy a. but u have to do so much stuff like so much stuff. 3 quizzes with no study guide so u have to memorize like 6 readings and 5 powerpoints for every single quiz. which turns out being a 20 page study guide. like 3 papers one is osm which is 20 pages long. as long u do the work u will get an A. i also never ever went to lecture.

I really enjoyed Mark's class. He was an awesome lecturer and the GSI's grade fairly. If you mess up, they usually allow you to do a re-do assignment for every major project. No midterm and no final!!

Class was structured using GradeCraft. Mark is a boring lecturer, but definitely clear and easy to understand. All grading is done by GSIs, who are helpful, approachable, and knowledgeable. Easy to get an A if you do the readings, listen in lecture, and complete the assignments. Only problem was the readings some weeks was upwards of 130 pages.

Mark's lectures are boring, and he is rather unhelpful. Also, it is his GSIs who grades the work, and they have ridiculously high standards. I am taking 400-level major classes and they were easier to manage compared to this one. THIS CLASS HAS A LOT OF LONG PROJECTS, ASSIGNMENTS, AND READINGS. DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS THINKING IT IS A "BLOW OFF"

The readings each week are so long and the quizzes assess if you memorized word for word one sentence out of the readings. The grading system is extremely confusing and not explained, the projects are ridiculously long and uninteresting. Unless you want to major in information, don't take it. Way too much busy work, but not very hard overall.

Not sure what some of these comments are talking about. Mark was a good lecturer and the class was interesting, easy, and fun. The readings are a bit extensive sometimes, but he reviews and summarizes them well in class. The GSI's were very responsive and helpful. Overall, I really enjoyed Mark and the class.

Not a bad lecturer but the GSI's grade ridiculously hard. I also did not learn anything since projects were really touch lecture material that deeply. Only take this class if you are applying to the School of Information, otherwise avoid the busy work you have to do when enrolled in this class.

Makes you doing a ridiculous amount of reading and quizzes can test any material from that. Crazy professor who puts way too much work in an intro class. Also GSI's have a crazy high standard. Do not take this class unless you're applying to the School of Information. Also speaks extremely fast in lecture and barely summarizes readings for quiz
The professor was wasted on this class. With the hype that surrounded Newman, he performed so poorly that taking this class has been my worst investment EVER!

This class is an intro level class. Very easy if you do the assignments and follow the grading criteria. A lot of "extra credit"/optional assignments offered so even if you don't do well on a paper you can make it up. The final was online and open note. There's also an opportunity to get an A+.

Pretty fun class, though a lot of it isn't knew. Lots of opportunities to get points, and it's pretty easy overall. Lectures aren't super exciting, but Mark is pretty much what you expect from an easy class. Nice, funny guy
Class is tough but his way of teaching is very interesting.

Mark is a brilliant professor! He structures his class to be engaging and accessible, which led to the best university lecture experience I ever had. All of his assignments and teachings are thoughtful and relevant; the right mix of concept with skills. The course is tough, but he is a true teacher, making it interesting while remaining helpful.

Dr. Threats was an entertaining lecturer but beyond that, I didn't love the class. The number of readings assigned was a lot and the grading rubric was unclear and arbitrary. Things were not graded quickly and it took over 2 weeks (at least) to get our final grades returned. Final grades were posted without all assignments even being graded.
Very accommodating and kind. I did not find the readings or activities to be particularly useful, and I was confused as to the purpose of the class.

Many group papers, all with same group throughout semester. My GSI was wonderful and made the course way more bearable. The lectures weren't very useful, and I was surprised at how delayed some important info would be. At some point, Melissa mentioned how our group projects aren't really even "contextual inquiry"...I'd prefer client-based projects.
too much homework, two exams and attendance required

Like all of Hess's classes, the workload is full 3-credit course. Assignments are vague, carelessly written, & demand mind-reading to complete “correctly.” Despite the lack of clarity, you're held to an unrealistic standard of perfection. Packed with busy work that felt arbitrary and unproductive. Feedback often felt petty rather than constructive.

Great teacher but *really* loads you up on homework, so it's more like a 3-credit course load. Midterm exam was over 3 hours long, that should be broken up. Syllabus doesn't have much detail, you learn about exams and things on an ad-hoc basis. Very helpful if you have questions though.

Also took 504. Hess is a great lecturer IMO, but his classes have way too much homework for their credit load. Creates intentional ambiguity in assignments for no reason. Expects you to know things not explicitly taught in class. Tough on grading. Heart is in the right place but needs to relax on the work and expectations.

This is 1.5 credit hour class, but had an insane amount of work, even more than my other 3 credit hour classes. The class wasn't too hard, but the workload was just too much. The midterm was extremely long. Also, sometimes when grading, they'd take points off for inane things.

Way too much work for a 1.5 credit class. Homeworks are written poorly. Midterm exam covered content not mentioned or elaborated on in class. Breezes over concepts via live demos in the terminal without taking the time to explain them well enough for comprehension.

The course was such a mess. Doesn't understand what a reduction from 3 credit hours to 1.5 should look like (the workload is supposed to be halved). Someone else should take over next time or Hess should stop only teaching 1.5 credit hour courses. The format was frustratingly chaotic as you had no idea what to expect any given week.

Insane amount of work for a 1.5 credit hour class, but instructor was very accommodating. The midterm/final were not difficult, but they did take some time. I reached out for additional assistance, and he took the time to find someone who could help me when GSIs weren't available. I learned a lot in the course.

Way too busy. Does a lot for the uni, but tends to bleed into his classes. 504 feels like a 3 CH class while it's only 1.5 CH. The content was very useful, but weekly homework, a midterm, and a final (and almost a project) is too much for a 1.5 CH class. The git portion is highly valuable material. Hess is funny during lectures and teaches well.
All his courses are wayyyyyyy too much work for the amount of credits. Pretty tough grader.

Best professor at the school of information he sincerely cares about his students and will go above and beyond to help them. SI 504 will teach you very practical Linux shell commands I highly recommend the course.

Great teacher and very purposeful in trying to help students learn. He keeps lectures interesting and the course was fairly easy and practical. 634 involved no programming where 635 required some programming knowledge.
Great lecturer, passionate about helping others learn, and very knowledgeable about Drupal and general IT.

I was disappointed with the lectures and content of the course. Many of the topics seemed pretty distant from the actual industry standard practice and even though he mentioned that we will dive into some technical aspect, there were no resources provided nor did we touch on it at the end.

Michael's teaching style and humor make learning enjoyable. I gained valuable insights into XR design and felt motivated. The teaching team is flexible in evaluation, focusing more on individual progress. While the course is a lot of fun, it's definitely challenging. If you're ready to push yourself, it's an incredibly rewarding experience!

Dr. Nebeling consistently provides constructive feedback and is incredibly resourceful, which makes the course very engaging. He always offers insightful feedback and responds promptly. I also appreciate how he brings guest speakers to class, offering us exposure to the XR industry in large firms!

Professor Nebeling is one of the greatest educators I know! I have 2 Master's in Education and worked as an instructor and curriculum designer for 8 years. I appreciate Professor Nebeling's pedagogical philosophy and teaching approaches! His course align learning objectives, learning experiences, and assessments very well!

Disappointed with the VR/AR class. The content was outdated, and the instructor was both incompetent and uncaring. Explanations were unclear, and the class lacked hands-on experience, making it a poor learning experience overall.

Michael Nebeling, though knowledgeable, is a negligent and uncaring instructor. He seems more focused on boosting his visibility and reputation than genuinely committing to the academic community. This makes students feel like stepping stones in his career rather than valued learners, leaving them disillusioned and dissatisfied.

Moses was a great lecturer for the class "How to Build a Killer Team" (now known as "Organizational Management in Startups"). He cared about his students and his lectures were engaging. Grading was a bit unclear as most assignments didn't have number grades, but checks for completion. The semester-long group project was fun and taught me a lot.

This class was the entrepreneurship practicum. Moses is very passionate about the class and all things entrepreneurship he's very smart and seems to always have good advice to give for your project. He does seem to be preoccupied with his own start up at times but overall is a good teacher worth taking

Prof Naseem is a real OG. He has one foot in academia, and the other in the real world solving problems, especially in Pakistan. So he combines scholastic content with pragmatic reality, which is refreshing. He makes proper adjustments during the course to maximize learning and minimize busy work. Grades fairly and makes you *want* to earn them.

The class I took with her was not enjoyable. We can't even reach out to our clients instead we have to do everything through her. Still unsure how she qualifies for this position, would not take again.

I took UX Driven Entrepreneurship with Natalie and absolutely loved it! Natalie is clearly knowledgeable about everything we learned and explained the material well. Having the opportunity to work with a client made the course so much more beneficial, I learned so much by applying the lessons to a real project. Definitely recommend this course!

As someone in both Ross & SI, this was hands down my favorite class! Natalie is an amazing teacher. She really understands the industry, takes time to answer every question, and explains things clearly. Though I'm not going into gaming, I left with three portfolio pieces. It perfectly blends strategy, marketing, and creativity in gaming lens.

Class is tailored to the video game industry, those who don't play games might not find the class as engaging. Useful for getting experience with UX research and general pre-production skills, may require self studying if new to UX. Instruction can be vague at times, but Natalie is very glad to help those who ask. Grades strict, gives extra credit.

Natalie is an amazing professor! I loved the UX-Driven Entrepreneurship course; it distills all the parts I like about UX Research into actually applicable learnings and methods, devoid of much of the overly-academic busywork that comes with most UX classes. If you're engaged in the material, the class is a breeze, just takes some dedication.

Natalie's courses, while good on paper, often fail in practice. With unrealistic expectations (like 60 user interviews in two weeks) and tough grading, what would be a very fun course becomes a nightmare for students. I like Natalie as a teacher; she does a great job of walking you through the content, but the criteria are unnecessarily difficult.

the most awful professor that I've ever seen. tons of attendance, no generative AI use(or you may be warned). All the things I learned were less useful than my other course, and l believe it is a waste of my time to study this.

Learned a lot about entrepreneurship from this class, which has been really helpful for UX research. The workload is a bit heavy, but the material is very comprehensive, and the lectures are worth revisiting multiple times.
LOTS of guest speakers... and lots of work.

Professor Fang did a good job with 311. Hopefully future students will get even better editions of the course, but I was happy with it for a first iteration. Improve the clarity of grading standards and requirements and you're basically there.
Finally some gaming in SI, and its good one but don't take it lightly.
Tough but very worth it. I liked the lectures and speakers, and Professor has a lot of games industry and entrepreneurial experience. How does she run a games studio while also teaching? Lol

Professor is ok. Teaching staff along with grading is quite ridiculous. You can lose points for PARTICIPATION. It makes no sense because did we not complete the "assignment" regardless? The GSI/IA's grade all of our work based off THEIR opinions too. It's insane since there's almost never clear directions stated for us. VERY messy class.
The professor was unhelpful in navigating conflicts in group projects. Attendance was mandatory even though it's a word-for-word repeat of the assigned readings.

Just don't. Awful course structure design, she makes the no.1 mistake in UX: trying to cater to everyone and ended up with no one satisfied. And because the course doesn't teach everyone much, so your rookies design teammates will still be basically useless at the nearing the end of the course where you could really use help in the group projects

Yes, they're right. She does read off the slides. This can be dreadful at first, but the nice part is that all the pre-readings she makes you do helps you understand the material so you don't really have to pay attention but you will have to participate in the in-class activity. As for the discussion, this is primarily done by the GSI!

Very, very dry lectures that are word for word from the readings. Grading is crazy; you need a 95% for an A. The GSIs taught most of the class and the professor was frequently absent from class. Such a shame since the material was actually pretty interestingthe format it was presented in was anything but that.

Knowledgeable in the field, but not a very good lecturer. Course content is interesting and relevant, but lectures are usually dry. Attending the class really doesnt gain you much because the lectures are mainly her reading verbatim off of the slides.
Get ready for lots of cold calling and other scare tactics to get you to participate
Really bad

Professor Ghannam was the best instructor I’ve had at MADS she would go out of her way to answer student inquiries and had amazing office hours. This course though was difficult like having the professor and GSI support made it manageable but it was challenging.
She is really helpful loved the course!!!

Professor Ghannam was one of the best professors Ive had at MADS because of how much she cares about her students. She would always take time out if any day to help answer my questions and patiently walk me through tough homework problems. The homeworks were challenging but I learned a lot.
Had Noha for NLP and really hope to have her for more courses. She always gave good and honest advice and really wants to see students successful. Really hope to have her for more courses. 10/10!

This class was part nightmare, part fun. One lecture and one discussion per week. Lectures weren't fun or engaging. Reading quizzes and in-class assignments made this even more horrible. Can't really miss class... ever without harming your grade. Projects were super duper fun though, which is the only reason why I give this class any merit.
Didn't even look up from his phone when talking to him during office hours

The "practice exam" given is significantly, significantly easier than the actual exam, which had an average grade of 70% (and he did not give us a curve). Professor was uncommunicative, both via slack and email. Insanely difficult class.
It's okay

Anthony is extremely friendly and passionate about programming! If you have prior coding experience, this course will be a walk in the park. In contrast to most introductory level programming courses (that are much more intense), the content is designed to walk you through step-by-step (the homework tell you verbatim what to do).

Do not go to his office hours-- you won't receive any help. Asks GSIs, and go to their office hours frequently. The main issue with the homework is the wording. It isn't that the problems are hard, but that the wording is so tricky that you cannot figure out what you are expected to do.

Kind of an "I'm mightier than though" attitude. Does not explain concepts well. Briefly goes over problems he made quickly before class and rarely gets through a whole lecture. Homework never makes sense because concepts are never gone through appropriately. The readings are ridiculous. He also believes everyone should go on to take 507. No thanks.

This course is not beginner friendly at all. My best advice when taking this course is to form a group of friends to collaborate with for assignments, go to the GSI's office hours, and go to the weekly labs to earn extra credit points. After the first few weeks, the homework is worded so tricky that it takes over 10+ hours per week.

Anthony is a super nice, very passionate, great guy, and mostly not helpful; stick to asking GSIs for help because Anthony *almost* exclusively delivers his guidance in riddles. Survival tips: 1. Go to the GSIs' office hours, not Anthony's (again, riddles and tangents). Go early. 2. Make reliable friends in class; you're gonna need 'em.

The class is designed to help students get a good grade - lab exercises are extra credit, lots of office hours, and HW is written with super detailed instructions that guide you to the right answer. Compared to most intro-level programming courses, which are usually designed to weed out students, you should be able to get an A easily if you try.

Professor contributes a lot to this course. He uses the latest materials for lectures, homeworks and exams. The final exam is meaningful and interesting. There is no curve for scores in this course and the criteria for the final grade is clear. You accumulate points by passing through a series of autograder tests. All you need to do is work hard!

The lectures are very hard to follow if you don't have programming background. I often got lost during the lecture and had to learn myself in order to finish the homework. What he teaches in lecture had a huge gap/disconnection with the homework.

Lectures may be a bit hard to follow, but he finds ways to genuinely make class interesting and find cool topics to discuss. I found his organization very good compared to other SI professors, and I always knew what was expected of us. He and GSIs were consistently available for questions and office hours, but sometimes his responses were confusing

This course was overly complicated for no reason. The only reason I was able to pass was from going to office hours 3x times a week. The lecturers were hard to follow, I often had to reteach myself the information or go to office hours. The grading scale in canvas is not synced with the syllabus. Canvas will show an A when you really have a B.
No Autograder plz That's a torture.

Anthony goes off on tangents and it makes it very difficult to stay tuned into the material. I find myself grappling with basic concepts because lectures and so all over the place. He means well and is enthusiastic but fails to set students up for success .

Anthony is passionate about the subject matter. But the quality of assignment are terrible. First, the prompt: this is a programming class there SHOULDN'T be tricks in wording of the questions. Anthony might thought he's funny but it's annoying. And the autograder is a disaster: minor style issues will fail you, and it won't tell you what's wrong.
Unwarranted arrogance. Lack of common courtesy. Unprofessional.

This was a really great class. Professor Edwards is really knowledgeable about the subject (history of computers) and is eager to answer anyone's questions. He is responsive to class feedback too. The homework and tests are pretty easy as long as you do the readings. If the subject interests you, definitely take this class. I learned a ton.

Absolutely terrible. Like someone else said, he changed the amount of points for the midterm after we took it. He also graded the undergrads like graduate students, imo, which, while admirable in an academic sense, is completely irrational, since we generally take more classes than grad students, and don't care as much, usually, about this subject

Terrible professor. He changed the point values of questions on the midterm after we had completed it. He made the extra credit mandatory. We watched a documentary in class which reinforced every negative stereotype I've ever heard about computer programmers. Computers were treated as more of a footnote than the focus. Very arrogant.

Yikes. Could maybe dress a bit more...appropriately. Also, treats the students like children when, in fact, we're in graduate school and can be presumed to have moderate mental capabilities. Asking us not to use laptops is fine, but at least make a consistent policy. Subject matter is okay, but we deserve more respect.
Lectures are clear and generally interesting but not usually related to the reading which is what is related to the first couple essays.

He's okay, but really pompous. Sends out an e-mail telling us not to chew gum in class because it's unprofessional. Yet he wears jeans and seems to have no problem with his colleague playing irrelevant music videos in lecture. Yeah, that's professional...
No Comments
No Comments

I took SI311 with him (was not an option listed hence IOE334) and he was awful. He did not grade any assignments until 3 days after the semester ended. Our class was supposed to receive feedback for our projects after submitting an outline, he never gave feedback yet graded extremely harshly. He was terrible. I would not take his class again.

Bro makes no sense in his lectures. Goes over useless stuff that's never tested. Everytime I open his lecture recordings I question why I'm even taking his class. No practice exams given, how are we supposed to study lol.
the sildes sucks, the structure of the class is weird and hard to understand.
A nice person, but not a good lecturer. The efficiency to learn things in his class is low. But he is really a nice guy if you reach out to him or ask questions in his OH

He is a nice guy but an awful lecturer. He does NOT explain anything and will pretend to not see your raised hand "for time" and just speeds through everything. His lecture slides are awful and if he just uses the blackboard (b/c he didn't prepare beforehand), it would be worst as it is even more unorganized. Also does not wear his mask properly.

Paul cares about his students & wants them to succeed but this doesn't translate to his teaching. He's unnecessarily picky with grading. You'll fix the mistakes from the last report on the next one and he'll find something else to complain about. It was like he just had a policy of not giving anyone a good grade and graded reports accordingly.

This class was so poorly organized. No materials provided and sometimes we were expected to buy materials which is really unreasonable. Grading was unnecessarily picky and even after going to office hours multiple times for a single report, up to 20 points were taken off of each report for really insignificant details. Avoid Paul at all costs

Please come to this class early and make sure to sit at the front. Professor Green often rambles, mumbles, and it's near impossible to hear him if you are outside a 2 foot radius of him. He's a nice guy and a pretty easy grader, but he is an awful lecturer. His office hours are particularly useless because you'll leave it being more confused.

IOE majors, do NOT take 436 with this guy. Lectures are boring and irrelevant to the homework projects. As for the homework projects, this guy deserves a medal for being able to write 10 pages of directions while still leaving students with absolutely no clue of what to do. Even if you like ergonomics, this class is simply not worth the annoyance.

Professor Green can be kind of rambling in his lectures, but he was effective enough that I always knew what I was doing in the class. He was very generous when it came to grading exams. He posted "lecture notes" that were mainly blank, so that was annoying. I think he is a solid choice, if not the best.

He is all over the place. The lectures are not that clear and can be confusing. He uses the same example many times for lots of different models and since there are so many models in this course it is easy to get them mixed up. He does not provide practice exams, practice problems, or study guides so you only have the lectures to study.
Nice grader, not really good lecturer.

Probably the worst lecturer I have ever had. He has never made eye contact with the class and is super specific about everything. Tests are super hard because he doesnt actually test on what you have learned, he just tests on whether or not you put the right things on your cheat sheet.

I don't think this is the Paul Green that teaches stats so beware of that when looking at other's comments. Very smart guy. Knows what he is talking about and can present it in a decently interesting manner. He got really busy about halfway through the semester though and homework was graded about a month or two after it was turned in.

awful awful awful lecturer... you learn nothing from him. hw is random from lecture... there is no consistency with classwork and exams... nice grader, but it will bite you later in upper level stats classes.... TERRIBLE Teacher
He know a lot about the topic but you have to know something as well. Easy to schedule meetings to discuss assignments.

Terribly boring class and his lectures don't help make it interesting, but he's a nice guy. Homework was reasonable and tests were hard but graded very easily. He also posted lecture notes with examples which were helpful.

His lecture slides is more clear than the lecture itself. But I recommend to go to his lecture because you get a better idea of what is going on. He is a very nice and generous grader. You just need to listen and do your homwork.
He is a very nice person but a horrible horrible lecturer. Very bad at interacting with students. Classes can not be more boring. Hard to keep up with the material because of his teaching style.

This is a boring class and Green is horrible at teaching so you learn very little. Exams pick out the tiniest details, but you can have a page of notes (2 for the final) so you could print out all the notes in very small font if you so chose. Grading is lenient which makes up for the horrible teaching. Don't take this class unless you have to.

Homeworks are difficult but he pretty much does them for you in office hours. Exams are either easy or graded very generously. Not a difficult class but I also feel like I didn't learn much. Not a bad professor but lectures are very boring and he often goes on tangents and teaches things not related to the class.
The class may not be that interesting. But the professor explains things clearly and the exam is not too hard.
i would recommend him

He is extremely picky. Often gives 10 pages of directions for a 3 page assignment. Sometimes can be very annoying. However, if you like ergonomics and/or computers, this is a good class. All lab work is outside of class and everyone ends up with a B or higher.

I had Paul Green about 5 years ago and really enjoyed his class ( which can't be said for most IOE classes I took). I think the attention to detail was a bit on the extreme/unnecessary side at times but you have to appreciate Prof Green's passion for what he does. I enjoyed the fact that the class was a bit more hands on than most.

Not a terribly difficult class, but be sure not to get behind...late penalties are rather severe. Tough grader (most people will get grades in 80's on assignments) but very rarely gives anything lower than a B. Large part of grade is subjective, be sure to go to class and participate...it will definitely help your grade. Very interesting guy.

took both classes years ago and did my senior design for him. great guy and very passionate about his work. i encourage anyone interested in his topics to stop and have a conversation with him - he's an amazing person!!

Comes off as a bit of a typical, arrogant, engineering professor at first, but if you make an effort to participate in class, you'll actually find him to be one of the better engin profs. VERY nit-picky on assignments (he'll measure margins with a ruler), but using his 12-page guide for report writing has helped in other classes bigtime!
This class is really easy, I highly recommend it, much easier than 334. No exams, only lab assignments, which can be frustrating at first glance. You will not get less than a B.

He is a nice guy and has been the instructor since the class existed. The grading is very hard and detail oriented, but that is suppose to be for our "benefit". Overall, I like him as a professor. I will be taking 436 with him in the winter.
Truthfully an enjoyable course and an inspirational professor. Would take the course again give the opportunity!

Professor Resnick is one of my favorite people in this world. He is like a father figure to me. Yes, his class is hard but its because coding is hard, not bc him. If coding isn't your thing don't take it out on this man. He played the fiddle for us and danced after each exam. He tells a joke at the beginning of lecture each week. Love this man

Professor Resnick's class is no joke. The tests feel like a series of trick questions and are very difficult to prepare for. The tests seem to test your ability to think algorithmically rather than your ability to execute what you have been taught. If you haven't taken coding before, this will be a VERY challenging class.

This was definitely a tough class, but it was also really interesting and I think Professor Resnick did a good job of making the material manageable. The final was much harder than the midterm, but in general if you put in the work the class was average difficulty. Very clear lectures, lots of examples, and the professor was very open to feedback.
Paul Resnick was a good professor, but sometimes he was awkward. During the general lectures - which were mandatory - I often felt like I was wasting time.

Professor Resnick's class was complex in the fact that 90% of the material we were responsible to learn was independent and on the online textbook. This is not the best way to begin learning a brand new language, in my opinion. We had a flipped-classroom style, which everyone who I have talked to about it did not like at all. Prerequisite

Resnick was a nice guy but not a great professor. This was an intro level coding class, and sometimes he would throw things at students were pretty advanced. Also, some of the problem sets were not clear on what you needed to do. The class itself was just unnecessarily difficult at times and I felt lost often, even with discussion and the book.

Don't waste time going to the lecture except at the end of the semester when conceptual non python material is taught. Paul is fine, but I learned the majority of the material through the online textbook. The class picks up in difficulty when we move on from RSI and start using the Jupyter notebook so be warned.

People should learn from this man how to create a course. Phenomenal! Lots of respect and admiration for Professor Paul Resnick. He is an extraordinary manager and an exceptionally pleasant personality. People don't realize how busy this man actually is before passing comments about him. He cracks some 'geeky' jokes in class too!!

This course was replicated for a graduate class but taught as an undergrad class. General concepts with no application to the real world. Grading is different and you are forced to use social media even though it is against your will.

Truly the worst professor ever. The class is incredibly unclear, and he doesn't even teach. You must learn on your own outside of class and lecture is led by GSIs. Could not meet with him once throughout the entire semester.

Arrogant and condescending (even though insists on us calling him just "Paul"), this professor clearly needs some training in getting his ideas across to people while respecting alternative viewpoints. Doesn't bother with homework, group projects, nor participation, he just grades everybody on midterm and final.

The class has clear grading criteria and Paul is obviously very intelligent in the field. The exams are easy if you actually do the homework on your own without looking up the answers online. He also offers extra credit and drops your lowest homework assignment. Exams and the final project are worth most of your grade.

Definitely have to work hard for the grade. Go to lecture and office hours as much as possible. Problems sets due every week and you should definitely study hard for the 2 exams. Final project is going to take a lot of time and he really doesn't help much when you ask. He just makes you figure it out. GSIs are super helpful in section though.

Yes, this is a hard class, but I'm so glad I took it as my first intro to programming, which is difficult to learn and even harder to teach. Resnick did an OK job with that. Take this class for actual learning and don't expect an A without working for it! Office hours were helpful and you learn a lot by helping/collaborating with other students.

I'm surprised by the comments below; homework problems were fairly easy, and he gave multiple extra credit assignments throughout the semester. Never coded before, never went to office hours and did well. As a social science person, this was the most useful class I took in college, so don't let other reviews deter you!

This class is not an intro level course. Spent 30+ hours on the project, and countless hours doing weekly problem sets. I will actually be happy if I get a C.... I have never felt so helpless and frustrated in a class.

This class was absolutely HORRIBLE. It is false advertised as a beginner's coding class, but it is extremely difficult to understand the concepts without some prior knowledge even if you put in hours of work and effort to try and succeed. Resnick is not a good professor in the slightest because he doesn't know how to teach at a beginner's level.

Hardest class I have ever taken. The work load is nearly impossible. He skips over the confusing and hard concepts. Tests are incredibly hard and don't follow previous practice exams. Assigned us homework, a final project, AND a final exam all in the last two weeks of the course. Worst teacher ever. DON'T take if you don't need to.

Genuinely cares about his students' success. Yes it's a hard class but that is because the content is hard not because of the teacher. Expect to work and not get an A, but if you go to his office hours you will do significantly better. Really admired him as a teacher and person.

Professor Resnick was a great professor. He made learning python very enjoyable. The textbook is online and is extremely helpful. He is eager to answer any questions students have and is a super nice professor. I learned a ton from taking this class!

Professor Resnick the GSI put a lot of work into making this class a good learning experience, and it shows. The exercises in the book are actually helpful and the main textbook is free. They are willing to meet with you and work 1:1 on problems you have. This class kicked my butt because I didn't use them as resource early enough.

This class is really hard for me so far. I have no prior experience with Python and the textbook was good until they just threw us into the actual program completely blind. I have no idea what's going on right now and I'm just praying for a C. The teaching is eh. You're in better shape if you've had prior experience.

I don't know why people are upset about this guy. He's genuinely concerned about the understanding and hence success of his students, he's a really great guy (albeit a bit shy), and an interesting lecturer as long as you make the effort to tune in. This is his first time teaching SI 106 (previously EECS 182), and he's doing a fantastic job so far.

EECS 182 is easy for the first half and helps get you up to speed in programming for the second half which goes all the way to making your own (simple) web application. It's not hard if you go to every lecture, if you get behind and don't go to office hours to catch up it is easy to start doing really badly.Every lecture builds to the final project

This is not an intro course. If you have not had computer programming in the past skip this class or you will spend a large amount of your time trying to catch up with the professor. Spent countless nights with the GSI's who were very helpful. Year end project was impossible and was extended because students could not get it to work. Very hard!

he was alright at explaning the materials and we had fun class activities. However, the three papers which determined your grades were graded really tough because a grader graded it. Definetly the toughest SI class i've taken so far.

Here's the truth about SI 182: If you are coming in with prior programming experience, the class is very easy and you will genuinely enjoy learning all that Paul has to teach. If you are taking this class as an introduction to programming, it's HARD. Still got an A-, but you really have to dedicate yourself to the cause. GSI's were helpful

SI182, ladies & gents, is NOT, I repeat, NOT an intro class to programming. He assumes prior knowledge to the class and if you have no experience, you literally find yourself racing to catch up. It's ridiculous to spend two class sessions teaching us the basics, and then diving into CSS and HTML when this is not even the class's focus! DONT TAKE.

The first paper was randomly assigned with no warning or informative emails. The semester Google Adwords project had little to do with the class and many students ran into problems. Overall very bad class and grading is sketchy at best. Do not take this class for fun! Don't even take it to fulfill an elective!
I'm surprised by all the bad reviews. Resnick isn't the greatest teacher in the world, but he's far from the worst. He genuinely wants his students to learn, and I would definitely recommend him.

There's better classes out there, this one is not worth the opportunity cost. I had lots of amazing classes with Leda Adamic, Paul Edwards, Drago Radev. This one, and Paul Resnick in particular, were the worst in terms of learning environment (condescending), feedback (none), and concepts (rehashed info from other domains/classes)

SI should not keep such people among its faculty. Paul is unhelpful and just plain mean-spirited which hardly promotes learning. I expected more serious consideration of classes and choice of faculty from a top school like Michigan. Only over-qualified students seem to learn from his class, or at least those already experienced adn tech savvy.

Do not take this class if you are truly a beginner in programming, this professor does not know how to explain the course material without the employment of the field's discourse. He will try to help you with the material, but it's useless if you are grappling with the basics. He fails to see that you need to understand the concepts, not answers.
Whether or not he intends to be arrogant and mean-spirited, he sure comes off that way at times. Nicknamed "Napoleon" by some...

Resnick is a great teacher who cares that his students learn. These people who are reviewing him poorly must be either lazy or stupid (or both). Yes, you have to work hard in his class to get a good grade, but this hard work is rewarded by real understanding.

While he seems to have students' interest at heart, he still manages to embarrass students in class when they ask questions by essentially inferring that the Q was dumb. Openly favors more tech savvy students while showing disain for those not tech savvy.
unfortunately he's still around
terrible professor

Psych 356 with Professor Shah is very interesting. Professor Shah is clearly knowledgable about the subject and seems very willing to meet with students after and outside of class.

She teaches Educational Psych but she does not really know how to teach this course. A bunch of information on the slides with no clear key points, have to spend a lot of time watching the recording to take notes. Exam questions are vague, she asks you to find research as evidence, but many of them she doesn't take it. She didn't say it ahead.

Priti is a great professor. Her presentations were all very interesting and she was always willing to give help to those who needed it. She was also very welcoming and her suggestions have helped make my transition from high school to college much easier. Overall a great teacher!
really funny and exciting to learn research result from.

Priti was very nice and the material was interesting. However, some of the exam questions were poorly written and ambiguous. Luckily, she offered lot of extra credit (clicker questions during lecture).
Dr. Shah is a really great professor. She is funny, enthusiastic, and kind. She is also very fair and provides EC opportunities. She tries not to be boring in lecture too.

In my opinion, one of the best professors at Michigan. If you attend lecture, pay attention, take notes, study for the exam, you will do amazing in the class. She is an amazing lecturer. The class presentations were really fun- it helped the learning process! Priti is the best!!!!!
priti is my favorite professor so far. great, interesting class. very enthusiastic about what she's teaching. easy A without a lot of work.

Priti is definitely one of the best professors at this university. SAfter our mid-term was handed back she stated that pretty much everyone had received A's and she was just going to double that grade for our final. A god-send amongst other professor's who put holes in your pocketbooks, GPA, and self-esteem. A MUST TAKE!!!!

Priti is the BEST. She understands that students are here to succeed, unlike most teachers who are out to make their students fail. As long as you do the homework, you will be fine. She is definitely one of the coolest teachers i have EVER had.
Amazing, very interested in her topic. Conducts research in psychology and is a very easy grader
Fun class, really easy, must take
P-Shah is the best!

This was a good class. I really liked how there was no textbook, only online articles (saved me a lot of money!) Priti is cool and understands that many of the kids in her class were planning on going to grad/professional school after college. This was a class where the student presentation projects were actually informative and fun as well :)
I love this teacher! She is really nice and understanding and really knows what she is talking about. Tests are straight forward and easy.

I had her for an amazing freshman seminar. She was approachable, helpful, and fun. The class was discussion based and interesting. She is extreamly fair and the class provided an excellent transition from high school to college courses.
Great teacher for Psychology of Thinking. Very pragmatic. Proactive teaching.

Professor Q Mei is a poor communicator and teacher. He does not know how to manage a class. His GSIs take months to check assignments and Qiaozhu does absolutely nothing about it. 60-70% of his students are absent for most of his classes. He does not care about his students at all and refers to them by the term "folks" even at the end of the term!
Cool!

The workload is doable and there were about 2 or 3 exams, about 10 or 11 homeworks, discussion assignments, and 3 projects. The lectures were informative and the in-lecture practices, as well as the assignments they were helpful as well. The class is late on grading and a bit crammed in.

This was my first programming class at UMich and definitely a step up from what I was used to. Grading time took a long time; however, it wasn't really an issue for me. A major positive is the extra credit, which gives chances to make up points. Overall, the class is challenging but fair if you put in the effort, and you should get an A.

All her grades are at least 2 weeks late, never on time, and you have to go to office hours to ask anything regarding your assignments. Every question has it's own form you have to fill out. Your emails won't be responded to, and you have to use Piazza to communicate. Her class format is the only good, but the discussion sections are the worst.

Her descriptions of assignments or projects is the worst, she takes 3x as much words to explain the same thing in much simpler contexts. Her TA's delay everything, they're all basically other undergraduate students who simply cannot afford to prioritize just her class over other stuff obviously. God knows why she won't hire GSI's.

Receiving participation credit for attending class is really easy, she is a really nice professor who doesn't discourage students from getting things wrong. There is also a lot of opportunity to improve your grade if the tests or projects don't go well.

SI 206 was a great class because it exposes you to a bunch of different useful topics, while it doesnt go super in depth, its a solid introduction for everything given the length of class time available. I did find that the lectures were sometimes not that prepared in the sense there was downtime sometimes due to issues.

Ericson was not a very good teacher. She knew the information herself, but that doesn't mean she knew how to explain it to students - especially people who are beginners or don't understand the concepts very well. She often just showed slides without explaining them and when asked questions gave very vague, unhelpful, sometimes rude answers.

I have seen some rate my professor talking about grade disputes but if you make valid disputes Professor Ericson will hear you out and she definitely listen to you, she is a great professor and truly cares about her students
This professor is among one of the worst professor I have ever had if the class is not required for you do not take her course.

I will be honest the class itself is pretty easy but this professor is so terrible at teaching. I have gone to Office hours, and it is so disorganized and she acts like she has never seen the projects before. Even when you try to grade dispute she will not listen. I am excited to never have to take a course with this professor again.
Awesome job

i dont even go to this school but i use the picture of this women for every picture coding assignment ive ever done her picture was provided in our class and i cant name a single time i didnt pull out barbara dot jpg for an assignment

Dr. Ericson is a wonderful person. Smart and a good lecturer. A great lecturer, actually. But the course is terrifyingly disorganized and I dispute many of my grades. SI 206 is the wild west of learning. Exciting and depressing at the same time. COVID may be a factor in this.

Prescribes an excessive amount of required assignments on learning platform her lab is invested to generate more data at the expense of students doing anything actually useful with their time. Assigned multiple assignments due during the one and only break we had during the semester. Terrible organization; grading and feedback takes forever.

Barb is very sweet and I love her as a person. She is very passionate about teaching, but the class feels like a zoo at times with how unorganized it is. The GSIs mostly run the show, but it seemed like there wasn't much communication between the whole instruction team. Class is an easy A (if you at least try), and there's TONS of extra credit.

I wish all of my coding instructors had been as great as professor Ericson. She is able to break down confusing concepts so students can understand it, slides are very clear, concise, and easy to follow. So long as you attended all the lectures, did every assignment and went to office hours there is no reason you won't get an A.

Very nice, keeps lectures interesting, doesn't surprise with unexpected material on exams/projects. We had 10 homeworks, 2 projects and 1 midterm. iClicker in lecture. Very good at explaining concepts. Sometimes unclear about criteria and requirements. Really not hard to get an A if you do the assignments and pay attention.

Definitely a great professor, but not the most caring or empathetic. I think she is misunderstood, and a lot of her jokes don't go over well with the class. I learned a lot, and I think she is very knowledgeable, but don't expect to get away with any BS in this class. Bring your A game!

timeline is seriously off. does not know how to speak to students like humans. for some reason wont post standard answer keys (makes the students write the answer keys then grades the students' answer keys or will post the answers but maybe to like 1 or 2 problems) the worst part is that i enjoy the class content i just do not like her at all
girl whatever
this professor hates answer keys and doesn't approve students' circumstances and doesn't know how to spell AND shes bad at her job and has a FAB pick a struggle pls

Refuses to post answer keys nor lectures — why? Lectures, okay — but answer keys? I don't think she's very good at teaching either. I actually think she's very smart but she just doesn't necessarily communicate effectively with students outside of academia. Good luck in this class LMFAO

I think Prof. S is insanely smart and knows a lot about technology, but I hated the way both her and Prof. Bui taught the class. This class had such high potential and could have easily been such an interesting and engaging course, but the lectures were so boring and unorganized that I genuinely dreaded going to lecture twice a week.

Sarita was constantly narcissistic and condescending during the lecture. Always felt like she didn't want to teach the class and had something better to do. Did not enjoy her teaching style and was the main pain that I had throughout the class.
Neither knew about the topic she talked about nor how to respect each individual. For the first time my life I found a person could be such educated and disrespectful at the same time.

Professor Schoenbebeck is routinely abrasive and condescending to students and/or the class as a whole in the lecture sections. Not sure if this is intentional or a product of something else; it is hard to get over nonetheless.

Sarita is really knowledgeable in the field of ethics, and is truly a very engaging lecturer. I learned a lot in her course, and enjoyed it. The workload was pretty manageable a few group projects and reflections. The lecture activities were also manageable.
She's mediocre. More a researcher than decent teacher. If possible, take the other SI 422 professor.

She's just alright. Clearly smart, but is generally unprepared, wishy-washiness and has no real class control. This course basically a semester long group project so, for gods sake, please choose wisely. Also: you will gain much, much more in this course if you take initiative in your group and also talk with your professor and GSI.

This is a new class but was incredibly disorganized. Lectures were extremely boring, disorganized and lacked substance. Professor often posted materials and assignments late. Instructions were often unclear and assignments often didn't connect to class. Attendance wasn't required but lecture materials posted after-the-fact were unclear.

The material of this class is designed incredibly poorly. An interesting subject is made completely mind-numbing and the assignments make little sense or connection to the coursework. If this class is offered ever again, I hope more attention is paid to making sure students are actually learning.
He cares about his students and is overall a cool guy

Cares deeply about his students and is always willing to go above and beyond to help them out. Has a lot of great experience working with civic and non-profit organizations, so his feedback is always valuable.
Does not care about his students. Was not accessible outside of class or to help. Ignores students concerns and does not help them resolve them.

I took his completely remote EDUC333 class in WN21. Prof Fishman is well-coordinated, organized, and understanding. He uses GradeCraft and all of your assignments are visible from the get-go; there's quite a bit of Perusall readings but not much else besides 4-5 projects total, but this type of workload should be expected in a class like this one.

One of the best classes I have taken at Michigan. The lectures are great and engaging and the material is very useful. I worked for an online education company the summer after this class and they were very impressed by my knowledge of the field. I really liked the way the class was structured and the game competition was a lot of fun.

Barry has forever changed my life, in the best ways possible. I learned more about my life from this one class than arguably all of the classes in my major. I'm blessed to have learned under this man.
One of the best professors I've ever had. Class was challenging, intellectually stimulating, and incredibly fun.
Barry Fishman is the best professor I have ever had. He kept the class interesting and exciting throughout. What a fabulous course and professor!

Class was Video Games and Learning. Very interesting class, with a decent amount of work. But all the work was fun. Class was run like a video game, with boss battles and stuff like that. Wish I could take it again! Fishman is AWESOME and funny and so nice.
Great professor, very knowledgable and helpful. Do expect to do a fair amount of work.

Sophia is amazing and awesome. She really cares about her students learning about digital fabrication and will go out of her way to help you with your questions and concerns. She's fair and understanding, and doesn't seem very strict about grades; if you work hard and learn, you get a good grade.

Her digital fabrication class is great (or so I've heard), but for the love of god, don't take her for Creative Programming. She's an expert coder, but she doesn't know how to teach a beginner coding class. She jumps in, doesn't explain things well, and when asked for help, practically does the code for you. Assumes you're keeping up. It's a mess.
Incredibly helpful professor who is excited by and encourages students to do great work. Great chance to explore new media in your own way with professor that's helpful and knowledgable.

Excellent professor! Sophia genuinely listens to her students, is very knowledgable, approachable, and willing to help. She gives students the freedom with projects to do something that they are passionate, and excited about. Stamps needs more Sophias!

She's new to Stamps but she's really friendly and helpful and a good teacher. She's so passionate and adorable - expect to do some pretty awesome projects in this class and have full creative abilities. I learned a lot about digital fabrication and myself in this class. Would definitely take again.

I took Professor Brenton's SI 407 class this semester and wish I took the other professor. She's so disorganized and absent-minded and tries to gaslight students into thinking everything's going well when we're all confused. I've never taken a class that had assignments and announcements so clearly written by ChatGPT. Avoid her class if you can.

Professor Oney was amazing! The lectures were explained so clearly and it's obvious how passionate he is. He goes out of his way to help students and is very understanding. The class was really interesting and I learned a lot. Would definitely recommend this course!
it was worth the experience

I loved Professor Oney's class! Although I was a beginner to programming and often struggled with some of the concepts, he explained things in a manner that was very clear. He also was extremely understanding when I was confused about concepts and always took the time to explain them until I understood. He made a hard class welcoming and enjoyable.

Professor Oney is a solid teacher. His lectures are dense, so be prepared to be attention. If you don't pay attention, be prepared for a lot of heavy textbook reading. Class is structured well and if you turn in assignments on time you will be ok. Both Professor Oney and the GSIs are knowledgable and ready to help you. I would definitely recommend.

He's super passionate and even more organized. Works really hard to help all students understand and makes himself available outside of class. Used his lectures as more of a review and focused on material in detail in discussion sections and in textbooks.

He organized the class extremely well. Definitely the most organized programming class I have ever taken at U of M. He taught us everything so we can work on the problem sets and the project. There weren't much that he expected us to implement that we didn't learn. Great guy and would take another class with him if he teaches anything else.

Steve is a good guy and he is very clear with the material. It's not too much work but you definitely need to stay up to date with notes and concepts otherwise you will fall behind quickly. He is very helpful during office hours and takes things slow which is great. I would def take one of his classes again.
Professor Oney is super dedicated in his teaching. Class is easy if you attend lectures. Discussions and office hours are super helpful.

You could tell how passionate of a professor Steve was and how good his intentions were. However, he was unable to get concepts across in lecture and everyone would leave lecture confused and not understanding anything. Luckily, there were many office hours which were extremely helpful, but I learned all of the information through the textbook.

Very kind, and super easy to get in touch with outside of class. He gives you all the materials, and it is up to you whether to use them. Super fair grader, and seems to genuinely care about being a good lecturer.

Professor Oney's lectures are really enjoyable. He makes learning Python very easy as he explains each step in detail. He provides lecture exercises (which are worth 1 point each) at the end of every class to make sure we fully understand the material for the day. The discussion sections are also extremely helpful, so do attend those!

Really great professor. He could be a little betters organized at times, but he is a fairly new lecturer so that's fine. However, he is incredibly approachable, not at all condescending or intimidating and wants you to ask questions. He is really a professor who genuinely wants to help and see his students succeed.

He's awesome! Took a class with him and he explained the lectures really really well. He makes it easy to understand python. Take this class if you are applying to the school of information, or planning to minor in applied stats. He's the best. He's willing to help if you have any problems, he's very patient, and caring too. TAKE IT W HIM

This class is truly a battlefield, in all honesty, she's well respected in her field and she knows a lot. but on the other hand she grades like a monster, nothing is ever good for her, instructions are so unclear and she will have you writing a research proposal in 3 days and make you decide on a project that you have to do for a month and a half.

I have never in my 19 years of life met someone so unable to give unclear instructions. Ask a question and be prepared to be left more confused. This professor does not provide clear instructions in her assignments, and when asked for clarification on the assignment, she is unable to provide good clarity. I do not recommend her class, as it doesn't feel like learning or improving on creative processes as an artist.

Her instructions are unclear and her clarifications rarely help. Her class is a product design class not a creative process strategies class you will be writing multiple page long proposals with annotated bibliography.
Class should be called sticky notes and storyboard. If u like that this 4 u.

this class was unlike any other stamps class i have taken. this is basically like a psych ux research course. name feels misleading. feels like professor has no respect for the arts, or artists. you will not learn anything about design, or any valuable techniques. i came in thinking i would learn how to better use figma. don't be misled.

I really enjoyed her class. I can tell she's really knowledgable on what she teaches.
