This is the worst course that I am taking in this term. You could tell she walked into class with no plan. She just rambled on nothing and we learned nothing. It is also hard to communicate with her because of her accent. I won't take any of her classes again.
Ben Green teaching SI 305 is sort of like LeBron teaching fundamentals to school children.

I was confused on the lecture material during class (partly due to not paying attention) and Professor Rosenblat met with me for 2 hours the week before the final exam to go over any topic and go through practice questions (I only needed 2 hours to clear up everything). Super flexible with assignments and so sweet!

Omg why is Tanya not rated higher? She is extremely caring and great at teaching. I took this course online due to COVID-19 so it was difficult to pay attention (to all my classes). She is very knowledgable about her field. If you are interested in pursuing research, speak up and she will be very supportive.

I highly recommend this course and Prof Rosenblat. Applying behavioral economics to case studies was extremely practical and something that I speak about in interviews often. Lectures were always interactive which made them enjoyable. Test were never too difficult and graded fairly.

Tanya gets a lot of hate but it's not her fault her class requires more effort than some SI courses. If you actually took some time to learn the programs/concepts used, and spent an hour or two skimming/prepping for class, it is easy to follow and do well. She is not the best lecturer, but she has tried to make class more interactive. Took 301 too

SI 425 was offered for the first time and I think the professor did a great job. The course has a broad range of topics which is good for me. The professor herself is very nice and understanding. I wish to have a discussion section so we can ask our R related questions there instead of trying to do it together in the class in a big crowd.

Maybe she is great researcher, but as far as teaching goes she is awful. Would not recommend taking her class at all. Learned more from 10 minute YouTube videos about topics than her 1.5 hour classes.

God-awful teacher. Glosses over complex and nuanced concepts in lecture and then expects us to know them in-depth for exams and homework. She insists on integrating her background in economics in the class, constantly. Get Dr. Chuck for this class if you can, he'll actually teach you a programming language and will make the class interesting.

The content of the course could have been interesting, but Tanya Rosenblat was completely all over the place. A lot of the concepts were not clearly explained and when people tried to get help, she tried to shift blame to the students. Would have dropped it if I was not required to take this class. She should not be teaching.
Has no idea what she is talking about. Is kinda lost in class.
Class is okay. Assignments can be a little vague and there are a lot of readings and projects. Make sure to show up and put in the necessary work on the projects and you should be fine.
She has a large amount of industry experience which allows her to bring good insight to her lectures. Very helpful as well!

Has a pretty serious attitude problem. Has vague standards for assignments and whenever you ask her the most simple and fair questions for clarification she always responds in the most patronizing and disrespectful manner possible.

I was so scared of this class because of the reviews. I also had no relevant background at all, so I was worried! This class is a lot of work (super tedious), but you can succeed in this class! Go to office hours with GSIs. There is also opportunities for extra credit. I loved this class and many of my peers are planning to take the advanced class.
520 is the worst classes I have ever taken in my entire academic journey

In class for three weeks and each week has been unpleasant. He's condescending and set in his ways. Overall he creates a hostile learning environment.

I have anxiety every day that I have his lectures. The learning environment is uncomfortable. Class expectations are unclear. He did not create a safe space for learning. He always randomly calls on students to answer questions and does not show respect. I am only two weeks in and I am sick of his sass. He is condescending, when anyone asks for clarity he ignores the question and shakes his head. When he gives directions, it is immensely unclear. His class is not flexible nor accommodating. GD was my undergraduate degree and know his lecture is awful.

If youre thinking of taking this class, it really gives you a good foundation for graphic design. Homeworks are time consuming and repetitive at times. Haven't interacted with vadim that much but elena is really supportive outside of class. Go to office hours and resubmit your work, those helped me out.

Vadim is a great instructor! He expertly communicated the basics of design and design philosophy to our class, alongside his co-teacher Elena, and their technical tutorials literally allowed me to take those new skills and immediately put them to work in my day job.

If you can waive the classes they teach, PLEASE do so. Many unnecessary steps for you to do while working on the assignments, but you still have to do it for the grades. The feedback I received for my design assignment are super general and meaningless.

The course content is useful in your later career development because this is an elementary course. What can you expect more from an elementary course other than building a solid background? But this course would have been so much better if we have a different professor who can explain things well and really care about the students.

Just remember that they are not open for discussions dont take criticism well. Also dont expect to do a cool/edgy project because they dont care and will make a scene in front of the class. This happens so often. This course is just for you to memorize all the design principles. Dont challenge the authorities and you will get a A.

I also graduated a few years ago and working as a professional, but my experience was so different. They didnt have any real experience and were so narrow minded. They even claimed a government website page to be fake because they didnt believe the issue in that article exist, and they stopped many students from doing projects for the same reason

Vadim uses generic slides and tries to think about what to say on the spot. He cuts students off and shames students for lack of participation (seemingly clueless to his role in creating an engaging class space). His attitude is quite pompous, and frustrating because the subject matter is fascinating. The delivery fails.

They never let you finish your thoughts and always cut you off because they think they know EVERYTHING about EVERY industry. Providing irrelevant suggestions and mocking an App, not the design but the App content, when they know nothing about the audience. They show no respect at all and create toxic discussion environment.

Vadim is the professor for some of the most important classes for the UX Design track (582 interaction design and 520 graphic design) His lectures are extremely un-engaging despite having interesting material. Most professors will respect your thoughts when he asks for audience participation - not Vadim. He is extremely dismissive.

I highly recommend taking all of the classes Vadim and Elena offer. Each one builds on the previous class, and they each course is designed to maximize your learning. There is not fat in these syllabi, it is all material you will 100% use when you get out into the real world. This was the best class I took at UMSI, hands down.

Vadim and Elena's classes are the crown jewel of UMSI courses. I have used what I learned in this over and over again in my job. They are smart, they will offer great feedback on projects, they are endlessly accessible in office hours, and they are FUN! Love them both, take this class, you won't regret it!

This class was a great combination of playfulness & learning, I have many great memories. The concepts taught helped me put my rationale into words & I still rely on them as a Design Professional. Approach this class with an open mind and you'll have lots of fun (SI520 is a pre-req). Vadim is very helpful and genuinely cares about his students.

One of the best class I have taken in SI, it transformed me from an engineer to a designer. The class has a great combination of graphic design theory and hands on exercises, both of which prepared me to become a designer. You should not miss this class and the advance SI616 if you take design as serious career. Love Vadim and Elena!

Vadim is a deep thinker about graphic design. I found his lectures very insightful and I still refer to his slides sometimes. The two lectures on typography were my favorite. He will give you the why behind design during the first hour of the class. The second half of the class is usually a laboratory session where you will be given simple tasks.

Their class is a lot of work, but I learned so much in such a short amount of time. It was probably one of the most organized classes I've taken and Vadim's lecture slides were top notch. The labs were very fast-paced, but Elena was always willing to answer questions. I came into the class with no experience and came out with a solid portfolio.

It would be naive to study with the mindset that one class alone will make you a master and simply treat learning as something that will get you an easy A. They have more than 10 hrs of office hour per week, the opportunity to resubmit each assignment, and if you want to excel then you will take full advantage of these opportunities to seek advices

Elena and Vadim go out of their way to provide design critic. They taught me how to package my designs the right way to make the most impact on the client with my deliverables. Highly recommend their base class of SI520 and the advance class SI620.

SI616 is a step further from SI520 and is more challenging but also fascinating. The rubric for more advanced graphic design work could be subjective but students are always to go to the office hours to ask for feedback and listen to different opinions. Vadim may seem unapproachable or intimidating at first but is actually very sweet and nice. :)

I had the opportunity to work with Vadim and Elena as TA for SI520 from 2016-2017. They gave clear and descriptive grading criteria for each homework. Students could keep perfecting their work through resubmission to get better grades, which added workload for them but was very beneficial for students' learning and applied to the design industry

I took SI520 in my first semester in MSI 6 years ago. It opened my door to the graphic design world with design principles, design theories and hands-on design skills. Vadim and Elena gave very detailed checklist for each homework and allowed for resubmission so students can improve their grades over and over again based on feedback on the homework

I took SI 520 and SI 616 with Vadim, as a student with no graphic design background. I can honestly say that years later in my career as a UX and Interaction designer, I still use the design skills taught in this class. This class might be challenging - but it is the good kind of challenge that helps you grow with major career rewards. :)

Im thrown off by the reviews here because Vadim & Elena explain concepts clearly, are willing to meet outside of the classroom, & give you many chances to resubmit work. I learned LOTS from 520!! It seems like the other students dont really understand how education works and they dont like putting in any real effort- dont go to college then...

Vadim and Elena never clearly tell you the assignment requirements. After you turn them in, they say," Ah, you were supposed to do this other thing(not stated previously). Resubmit or you fail " They will make you resubmit 2-8 times with no clear feedback. They are obnoxious and do not care if you've opinions or a life. Please opt for another prof.

The only reason I did well in this class is because I've previously had graphic design experience. Otherwise, the professor doesn't teach you well enough for you to understand all the Adobe functions used in class. Also, there's a ridiculously large amount of hw that's way too much for just a 3 credit course.

I graduated 6 years ago from SI and I can honestly say SI 582, 520, and 616 were the most worthwhile courses I took at Michigan. I'm now a design professional and am very grateful that I was able to grab a spot at Vadim and Elena's classes. All the homework I did contributed to me getting my internship and full-time offers.

Took SI520 & SI616 offered by Vadim & Elena. Great foundational classes that help students bridge design principles, schools of design, and philosophy to practices. Dont expect Masters degree classes to spoon feed technicalities on Adobe suite. One can easily learn those from YouTube. These classes offer what is important to elevate design.

The 3 classes I took with Vadim and Elena were honestly my favorite my whole time at SI. 582 was such an amazing foundation for understanding the design process, while 520 and 616 made an exciting intro to graphic design. I developed so many valuable skills. They clearly care a lot about their students, and their classes are very rewarding!

Hard class but great class. I had a background in design and think the professors do such a great job condensing such an extensive field into an efficient and effective overview course. They're also kind and are always willing to go out of their way to help students which is refreshing a necessary for such a dense course. Recommend 100%.

While this class is not easy, I've learned a great deal of not only design theory but how to effectively use Adobe design tools and I say this as a complete novice. Elena and Vadim are very thoughtful in their lectures and Elena was always so helpful during office hours.

SI 616 is a bit challenging if youre not the most creative person, but Im glad I took this course... Its definitely helped improve my visual design skills! Prepare to have more creative freedom here than in 520. Fewer projects, but they require more effort. Overall, Im happy I took the course. The team provides constructive feedback!

Elena and Vadim put effort into their lectures, and make themselves available to help students outside the classroom. I especially loved that you could resubmit your assignments multiple times to earn a higher grade; this was a productive way to learn and do better! I enjoyed their SI 520 class so much that I later took SI 616 with them.

This course was great. Both professors gave excellent feedback, and the course design was well thought through with hands on project work making it easy to connect the concepts we learned in class to practical applications. Workload is significant for a UMSI course. (Consider it the equivalent of a lab, with more hands on time required).

I had no background in design and this class made me improve so much. It gave me a solid foundation in graphic design. Both professors are very direct in their feedback but I liked that. I knew I was not doing great without having to guess it. . Both professors are really dedicated and take time outside class to help you improve.

I took SI 520 with Elena and Vadim when I was figuring out that I wanted to be a UX Designer. They not only taught me all of the baseline visual design skills I needed; their willingness to provide feedback on multiple iterations of my designs taught me how to be a continuous learner and built my confidence, allowing me to pursue a creative career.

Not an easy class but a great one! UMSI is basically forcing an entire field to be taught in a 15 week time span so be ready for heavy workload & fast pacing. I had a degree in graphic design & still had to budget my time well to keep up. The principles & skills taught are incredibly valuable so soak it all in & enjoy learning new respect for art.

I took Graphic Design at the School of Information w/ Elena over 10 years ago now. Though Michigan SI is a fairly research-heavy program, this class gave me the visual design basic skills I needed to become a UX designer. As a design manager @ Google now, I owe a great deal of my career to Elena's expertise and the rich exercises in the class!

Terrible class I sincerely regret taking the course with Elena and Vadim. During the covid-19 pandemic they have no patience, care about students less, and will not support you any way possible. They go out of their way to knock you points and do not explain anything. Do not take!

Worst class I have taken because Vadim and Elena do not know anything. Do not take this class! They make up grading criteria, they do not follow the rubric. They do not care to hear your thoughts. They lecture with no textbook or citations they just make stuff up.

Unclear grading criteria and terrible feedback were what made this course so hard.

The only reason I knew how to do anything in this class is because I've previously had graphic design experience. Appreciated the fact that he set up so many ways to access the class material (power point, podcasting) especially since we were working without a textbook for half the course. Knew nothing about computer programming, but succeeded anyway.
Very helpful and explains things clearly.
I am pretty much in love with Dr. Chuck. He is far superior to most of the instructors I've had at SI and does not waste your time. Plus he is kind of a DILF, but maybe that's just me.
Great prof.
Went above and beyond to help students learn the material. Including lecturing from Spain.

An excellent professor! Lectures were fun and informative, and he's great about responding to students' questions. I learned so much from his web design class, and he made the material interesting. Definitely one of my favorite classes at the School of Information.
Great class. The professor has a lot of knowledge.
Fun, patient and flexible. Great at getting students to share their knowledge and ideas as well. A little slow with the grading but his easy-going attitude definitely makes up for that!
Great introduction into python, a drive in and start working style made it great.
Dr. Chuck has endless patience for his students and enthusiasm for the subject matter.
No Comments
Dr. Chuck is very invested in his students' learning, which is what makes him awesome.

Do not recommend this class. Very limited value content-wise since the majority of it was self-explanatory. Grading was extremely rigid (mostly done by GSI). Harsh deductions for minor details made it hard to get full credit most of the time. Professor's expectations were overly strict given the relatively low difficulty level of the content.

Great professor. This was one of my first technical classes, I came from a different background. He was more accessible outside of class for any questions I had about homework or ideas I wanted to pursue. He wants the students to succeed in the program and proposes changes to the curriculum that are better for students. Overall would take again

One of the best instructors at UMSI - caring, sharp, and truly knowledgeable. He genuinely listens to students and is always willing to help. The material is valuable and engaging. While the pacing can be fast and the homework time-consuming, it's absolutely worth it. Highly recommend this class.

2nd semester of the UMSI data analytics track is extremely challenging. Dr. Teplovs very much respects his students and cares about making the course better for them. He takes students' concerns very seriously and is willing to make course changes. Show up and do the in-class work and you will do well. Has great, informative lectures.

Solid course and professor. Weekly homework assignments and a course group project split into 3 parts. Effective use of lectures with applicable content. Project is open ended, so creativity welcomed. I don't really have anything negative to say for the most part!

He teaches in a way that prepares you for a career. Instead of limiting students to a few things covered in class, we could use any libraries and methods to conduct analysis. He encouraged us to use copilot. Day 1 he posted videos on all topics so we could use things we haven't covered yet. Very nice, understanding and explains concepts clearly.
Insightful, caring and down-to-earth professor. I learned so much from his course that I will be able to use in my future career. Truly a great teacher and a great human being.
Prof. Teplovs is a very good lecturer and is a strong advocate for students. He's both thorough and accommodating. This is the type of professor most people really want, I think.

Chris is an amazing lecturer! He is so nice and approachable and really cares about his students. He is very open to feedback from the class if the material is moving too fast. I learned a lot and am taking another class of his soon.

Dr. Teplovs is a nice guy, but it feels like this class is a low priority for him. He definitely cares about his students, but the class is a lot of copy/paste. Pre-recorded lecture notebooks where we just run the cells and watch the video. I would rather have to do more work for a technical course. Not a difficult A.

I learned so much in Prof. Teplovs Data Manipulation and Analysis course. Teplovs is an amazing lecturer--he really cares that students understand the material and walks students through the in-class lab so we're ready to do the homework during the week! Teplovs really supports his students and wants us to do well & explore the course topics.
How does Professor Teplovs not have a 5.0? Hes the most kind, understanding, insightful human being on earth!

Attendance isn't taken, but going to class is necessary to understand the material. Each class, we work through a pre-made Jupyter notebook together, and answer questions at the end. I didn't retain/memorize as much as other classes, but he emphasizes the importance of keeping notes. Gave us 2 extra days for the midterm - awesome guy, super caring.

Professor Teplovs is my favorite professor. I had him for SI 110 and he presented the material in the most insightful way. I had medical problems during the school year and he was the most genuinely caring and empathetic teacher. As long as you learn to schedule the long assignments out ahead of time, then you will be fine. Lectures r worth going 2

The structure and grading criteria are really complicated, but the class itself isn't too bad. There is a lot of work (essays), but it is graded pretty easily if you follow rubrics. There are a lot of readings, but you can get away not doing them sometimes. Lectures are sometimes dry, with a lottt of detail/information. Prof is a nice guy tho.

Prof. Teplovs teaches to prepare you for the real-world in SI 110. Data science, information management and use in technology and everyday life with traditional information applications. very practical information for all fields but especially so if you have any interest considering SI fields or applying to the school.

Ok professor to be honest. Very boring lectures and honestly the course hw/projects were way too easy. Didn't cover pandas (a typical data science topic) and taught us outdated methods. Also didn't show up to class for the last week for some unannounced reason but regardless an Easy A. Just would've appreciated more instruction

The professor was consistently unresponsive, rarely replying to emails or addressing student concerns. Their lack of communication made it difficult to understand course expectations and left students feeling unsupported. This unavailability severely impacted the learning experience and caused unnecessary stress.

This class had potential to be good but wasn't. This was a ULWR but while trying to teach you coding at the same time it's doing too much. Christian was unable to find the balance of teaching us writing skills and teaching us coding. The lab assignments were mostly over thirty pages long and only worth 3 points. The paper prompts were also boring

Professor Sandvig really cares about his students and prepared great materials during Covid. Materials are a little dry and the class definitely isn't easy (especially for those new to programming), but he provides all the tools you need to succeed. I didn't even want to take this course, but I ended up learning a lot.

This class has completely turned me away from SI. The class is a 2-hour lecture that has the most boring material in existence. The professor also randomly calls on people because people rarely participate (because the class is unengaging!) which makes me feel like I'm in high school again. I don't enjoy his style of critique either.

An authority in his field. Cares about students and is very "present" in the classroom. Looks for student opinions on touchy matters. The class is set up for you to succeed and presents many opportunities to earn credit and learn. Be sure to study for exams, lots of good concepts to know.

The worst decision I've ever made. This class is the worst. Prof is nice though. The assignment rubrics are vague, and the GSIs grade insanely harsh for no reason. I spent more time suffering through this class than all of my upper-level classes combined, and it was never worth it. DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS UNDER ANY CIRCUMSTANCES.

Dr.Lampe is very clearly passionate about his study and his enthusiasm will undoubtedly rub off on you. If you develop good rapport with him, he is eager to help you and guide you. His ability to form relationships with his students and connect with them is impressive for how large his courses are. I highly recommend any class he instructs.

so much work. need to participate on discord and go to lecture and discussion to fulfill weekly engagement points. tests were difficult and it was every other week. lots of readings. big assignments almost every week for like 13 weeks. lecture boring, but interactive. would not take again.

SI110 requires far too much work for being an introductory course. Lecture is pointless since exams are exclusively based off reading assignments, however poor attendance can drastically harm your grade. Overall not an awful class and the material is interesting,wouldn't recommend if you're not sure if you want to pursue information science/UX

Professor is nice and funny during lectures but SI 110's class structure is terrible and way too much work for an introductory course. The projects and almost weekly reports are graded rather harshly and very tedious. The exams aren't easy either. This course genuinely made me reconsider if I even wanted to pursue the UX minor. Avoid if you can.

One large project over the course of the semester but some other pretty intensive assignments as well. A lot of reading so the course often feels overwhelming. Lowest test and paper scores are dropped and not all assignments are required though

The class simply is too much work for a 4-credit course with the new style. The old reviews reflect the past model of the SI 110 course- the new one created during Fall 2025 has 5 content-heavy exams, 4-6 in-depth written essays, as well as a 5-part in-depth project throughout the year, with mandatory attendance. Professor is also weird egotistical

Do not take this class. I have never had so many assignments at once. The tests require a full week to study, are every couple of weeks, and even then, the concepts are not always included in the lecture; some are just about readings. Keep in mind that one of 8 readings assigned this week has 200+pages in it and this is on top of everything given.

Prof Lampe is my favorite teacher so far. Genuinely funny guy and is knowledgeable. The class is really not as bad as everyone says. Just be organized, keep track of assignments, and you'll be fine. The class is a point/gamified system. You only need 935 points for an A and you have the chance to earn over 1000 points with the extra credit.

Class has a lot of papers and readings, but the material is very interesting. Go to Cliff's office hours and make sure you read the syllabus and you will be fine. Assignments won't be explained to best so it's up to you to figure it out. Aside from that, the teaching team and Cliff are absolutely amazing.

Dr. Lampe is super cool! SI110 is super time-consuming though. A few large projects and lots of writing. The content is super interesting though and Dr. Lampe seemed to genuinely care about what he was teaching, which is always refreshing. Lectures can be a little boring, but Dr. Lampe does his best to integrate cool "nerdy" things and jokes often.

I absolutely loved this class! It was very informative and interesting. Professor Lampe is a great lecturer and very helpful. The workload is definitely doable, it can be slightly overwhelming but it is still a very very great class to take. I highly recommend!

Professor Lampe seems like a nice guy, but the lectures often felt like a waste of time in the context of the class. More essay-heavy than my upper-level writing class, grading was all over the place due to bad rubrics. How well you did on an essay was pretty much based on who was grading your paper. Super frustrating course to try to do well in.

Crazy amount of writing, way more than any actual writing class that I've ever taken. Professor doesn't respond to emails and assigns many readings. Definitely take this class in a semester where you have more time.

insane amount of papers and the grading criteria was never clear/seemed subjective. I did good but didn't like that the professor and teaching team made it seem impossible to get higher than a B all semester. lots of readings but you can maybe get away with reading only when you have to write the papers

I love Lampe. He's inspirational, knowledgeable, makes huge chunks of information not intimidating but very digestible. It's clear that he cares a lot about his GSIs and PhD students. His class is the only class I would show up to take notes furiously. The workload is A LOT, high expectations, take his class in a semester where you have more time.

I signed up for SI 110 during the 3 week add/drop deadline, and it wasn't too hard to catch up. There is a lot of writing in the class. The readings are only helpful for writing the essays. No quizzes or exams in this class, and your grade is based on the essays and group project final. If you do the minimum for essays you will get an 88% avg.

Dr. Lampe was great. He's very knowledgable in his field, and very funny and interesting. However, the class, SI110, was awful. There's a crazy amount of work for a. 100 level introductory course. 50-75 pages of reading per week, tons of essays, multiple massive projects, combined with the fact that the field of "information" seems kind of made up.

Not a fun class. Project and paper rubrics are super vague and and GSIs like to give round about answers to questions, making things even more confusing. An 88% is considered "meeting expectations," and to get above this you must "exceed expectations," whatever that means. You will spend a tons of time on papers and not get the grade you deserve.
Cliff has so many years of experience in this field. He's super knowledgeable and the class is super engaging and enjoyable. He provides constructive feedback on assignments which helps you improve!

Dr. Lampe's class was one of my favorite's last semester. SI-110 is not a hard class to get an A in if you follow all the guidelines and details posted in the rubrics. There are a lot of readings but they are necessary to do well on the quizzes so take good notes.

Lots of readings for class they do help you understand the concepts well, not 100% necessary but help a lot. Dr. Lampe keeps lectures funny and entertaining as well as engaging outside of class, in dicussons and virtually. Talks a bit fast so keep up but not necessarily all vital information to write down or anything.

Lots of papers, lots of reading, but it's all very very interesting and Professor Lampe has put himself in a position of having a lot of experience in subjects that are just now starting to get noticed. The class is a lot of reading, but fairly straightforward to get an A on. Lampe is incredible.
Prof Lampe was really great! He is super understanding and is really passionate about what he teaches. It makes you interested in what he is teaching. The content itself is also very interesting.

Definitely the most interesting class I've taken so far, so if you're passionate about tech and the ethnics behind it this class is for you. Lampe has a great sense of humor and makes class entertaining. GSIs can grade a little tough but overall great class.

Such a good class. Lampe is engaging and clear. GSIs can be tough graders and unclear sometimes though. A lot of reading but the quizzes aren't too bad. Easy A if you don't procrastinate on the projects and talk to your GSIs beforehand.

Definitely recommend taking a class with him. He's funny, fair, and responds to email in a decent amount of time. He gives a lot to read, but it's doable if you manage your time. Projects and class discussions were fun and interesting.
The GSIs are harder to deal with than Professor Lampe himself. Coming in interested in SI, decided to drop it because of this course.

The GSI's are horrible graders, their feedback is not clear. I don't understand how they grade these assignments. Be prepared for a lot of reading and it will not be helpful for the quizzes. The lectures are very boring. It seems like a lot of the topics are random and do not connect to each other. I wouldn't take this class if not needed.

Professor Lampe stepped up after our previous professor couldn't teach anymore for personal reasons, and he's been nothing but fair and accommodating. Yes, there were quite a few readings but you really just had to skim them. The tests were also curved so as long as you put in a little effort you'd do well. The final project was also manageable.

AVOID. Too many readings. Too complex of readings. Project w/ absurd amount of work (expected to be 3500-4000 words/person in the group). does not respond to student concerns. tests are so hard (due to vagueness of class). seems like he's purposefully hard to be a cheeky U of M prof. annoyingly arrogant for someone from the west side of michigan.

Absurd amount of work. 100 + pages of reading per week. obscure pop quiz every week. 10 2-4 page essays, 2 projects equivalent to 10 page essay, another 2500 word essay. no assignments relate to actual material you need to know. will ruin your life. required to go to out of class lectures to complete essays. will be biggest regret of your life.

Absolutely terrible class. I was excited to explore the School of Information, and after taking this class I for sure will not pursue that interest. Guidelines for class are incredibly vague, the GSIs are hard graders. Expect to do an insane amount of reading and writing for this class. Lectures are EXTREMELY boring.

The readings each week are so long and the quizzes assess if you memorized word for word one sentence out of the readings. The grading system is extremely confusing and not explained, the projects are ridiculously long and uninteresting. Unless you want to major in information, don't take it. Way too much busy work, but not very hard overall.

There are weekly readings that are extremely boring and dry. Each week, there is a quiz over the boring readings. This class is "gamified" so grading is like a game with a leaderboard and points. The semester is almost over and I am just starting to understand how the grading works. Confusing. Boring. Awful. Pain in the ass. Dr. Lampe is very funny

Ironically, for a class built on gamification, this class is designed really poorly. Ten 2-4 page writing assignments (which you get little to no helpful feedback for your grade). 2 large projects, 1 semester-long project, weekly pop quizzes (on specific details from the 4 hours worth of reading), final exam. Don't take this. HIGHLY SUBJECTIVE

Cliff uses Gradecraft for his class - a program that attempts to turn an academic course at a major university into a video game. The course is structured in a way that is made to let students work at their own pace and instead the teacher drops all of the assignments on you at once. He has planned this class very poorly and I was jk about him hot

The Citizen Interaction Design class was great, it widened my perspective to new design problems in the world. The learning from this course will stay with me throughout my life. Best class of my first semester. I just loved the class and the professor. I want to take this class again.
I liked him well enough, and the class was interesting, but he seemed really arbitrary, and kind of egocentric.

Expectations at times are absurd and unrealistic. On one hand, he fakes empathizing with students and claims he understands how busy we are, while at the same time gives us grief for not doing the readings for one week. Our midterm was a MONSTROSITY of a research paper (15 pages and 20-40 sources!!). Funny, good lecturer, field expert, hard af.

Honestly, did the class take some effort? Yes. Was the grading team evil and did everything to stop you from getting your precious A? No. Cliff was a great professor, always enthusiastic and funny, which made the material a breeze. If you play by the rules of his game and trick yourself into thinking it's "fun," you'll be fine.
I love Cliff. Also informatics. Also the teaching team. Also gamification. Basically perf.

Hardest class ever taken, feels like a 6 credit class instead of a 4 credit class. Impossible to get an A no matter how hard you try. Grading is subjective, tons of reading. Painfully hard, emotions are constantly manipulated. If you end up taking this class, be prepared to pray every night to pass this class or get a B.

Took the class thinking it would be easy and fun, but the curriculum got super revamped. Crazy amount of readings and deadlines every week, which means crazy slow grading(literally 3 weeks) so you get feedback on what to improve after you hand in the next big assignment. Class is fun, but it was poorly revamped; it might change next semester.

He dramatically changed his class this year so most people get a B+. Even if you work really hard and do exactly what they ask for, they will give you an 88%. You also have to do immense amounts of readying and are quizzed on it weekly. Don't take this course unless you have to or are interested in the material and willing to get an average grade.
Lampe keeps his lectures interesting and has a great sense of humor. Cool material and truly inspirational professor who cares about his students
Funny optimist who cares.
Hilarious. Extremely well versed in the material. Will keep you interested. Gamification is give or take depending on your interests.
Incredibly easy class, everyone got at least an A-. Readings not required. Lampe swears alot in lecture to keep students interested, but it gets old pretty fast.

Great professor, I always went to lecture it was enjoyable and always hilarious. There are some really big projects and make sure you do Explorer Reports from the beginning so you don't get stuck trying to do a bunch of them at the end. Doing the readings weren't necessary, overall a fun & semi-easy class.

A great professor and a great class! "Dungeonmaster" is hilarious, and class was a pleasure every day. He's just a really good guy. There are some big projects, but they're interesting and you get to personalize them to fit you.
He's actually soooo funny and really entertaining. I honestly almost never pay attention in any classes but he's always able to keep my attention. Not a hard class at all.

Lampe is an awesome professor, you will never laugh harder in another class. Amazing sense of humor, as well as engaging material. This class is a great time and I never mind going to lecture. This professor will be one of your favorites you take at michigan!

eCommunities was a great course - definitely take it instead of 429 if you get a chance. Lots of weekly readings but they can be skimmed. Technical write-up of an online community each week, midterm project and final paper (no exams). Cliff is engaging and the funniest prof I've ever had.
Most hilarious professor I've ever had. Lecture is a lot of fun. The class is easy too, and the assignments are really engaging.

Great Professor who is well known in the school of information but still teachers an introductory course. SI110 is a pretty easy course and he manages to make the slides and lectures really interesting. Sometimes he makes grand claims which aren't exactly true but for the most part he is a great and funny teacher.

By far the best teacher I have had at the University of Michigan. I absolutely loved his breaks halfway through lecture where he would enlighten us to "Geek or Nerd Culture". Incredibly funny, and just a genuinely nice, friendly guy. If you want a great introductory class that is engaging and flat-out funny, take Introduction to Information.

Dr. Lampe is hilarious and his class is extremely interesting. None of the assignments are too difficult (and graded easily), and most are open note. He also posts all the lectures on Ctools as a podcast so you can hear as well. If you're thinking about taking this class at all, TAKE IT! You will not regret it.
Really funny guy. I looked forward to going to his lectures. He tries to tie back the course material to real life and wants his students to learn valuable information not only related to the class

Don't know why people are saying it's difficult. There's no need to do the readings. Just form a study group before the exams and it's an easy A. There's 80 POINTS OF EXTRA CREDIT. GSI's grade both of your papers very easily. Lampe is very tech savvy and the funniest teacher I've ever had. If you take this class, make sure it's with him.Very easy A
Entertaining professor, and explains things pretty well. However, the exams can be tough and they require a lot of studying.

Unless you're genuinely interested in information studies, I wouldn't take this class. Prof. Lampe, while amusing, goes off topic a lot. His lectures never feel like they have any continuity, much like the course. There are a lot of dense readings which are never really discussed in class or used in any assignments.
Very good lecturer and fun class. Random attendance taken during lectures but offers a lot of extra credit. Definitely possible to get an A.
greeeat professor. genuinely enjoyed going to class and listening to him speak. he does do random attendance in lecture sometimes, but all lectures are online. great class.

Several extra credit opportunities. If you do well on exam 1 and 2 and do the rest of your assignments, you can take a 0/100 on exam 3 and still get an A (what I did). She's super nice and caring. Amazing prof. Would take again.

Colleen is an extremely kind professor, she always makes sure no one is confused and questions are answered. As far as teaching in class, she assumes you know a lot so definitely stay on top of readings if you actually want to understand what she is saying. She offers a lot of extra credit at the end of the semester so your grade will be ok.

(SI 101 used to be called 106) Colleen is the best and cares about her students. shes reasonable, understanding and would always give extensions on our assignments. Readings are helpful since lectures can be fast but many did fine without. 3 exams, 1 makeup date, 1 final project(group or alone), 3 'HWs', weekly quizzes and lecture activities(easy).

Course could be more organized, but overall it's not that bad. You might have to work a bit harder for a few assignments, but if you're a CS major taking it as an elective (like me), the workload should be much lower than what you're used to. Colleen and the GSIs are nice and try their best to help out too.
This class was not bad, but the workload is a bit too much. In addition, lecture was kind of confusing at times but overall, I definitely gained some knowledge.

the most rude professor i've ever had. she puts up a sweet face but is never there to help. she's also straight up mean cause she knows she has the power and the student can't do much. save yourself some trouble and don't take her class.

SI339 shift to CPDs was a disaster. The class was chaotic, unorganized, and confusing for both students and staff. OH were unhelpful, as GSIs and TAs were always confused themselves. I spent more time decoding assignments than learning, focusing solely on meeting deadlines rather than understanding the material. I don't recommend this class AT ALL!

she's a nice person. that's basically the only good thing. everything about this course was a mess and the gsis also didn't know what was going on. this class would have been easy if it was organized properly but instead i fought for my life to get an A. i don't think colleen knew was going on half the time either. never take this class.
disorganized and does not know how to simplify concepts. be ready to self learn.

This class has amazing grade/work policies to make the class a low-stress enviornment for students to learn Python. She designed the class to be very lenient and forgiving, we get to drop our lowest exam scores. Anyone who says this class is stressful has a skill issue

On the first day of class she proposed an O/U for how many times she would fall off the stage at the front of the lecture hall. Absolutely goated professor. If you know how to code this class is very easy if you don't it should also be pretty easy. She knows her stuff fr.

106 with Colleen was my favorite class this semester. I learned so much about Python, and she structured the course so you can fail over & over & still pass easily. It makes for a super great, learning-focused environment. I found a new love for programming and Colleen is such a great person and instructor. Would recommend taking anything with her.
colleen is a talented instructor, i wish i can come back and the class again. how she explains different algorithms, is awesome. i will recommend here over and over again

Colleen is one of the best professors I've had here at umich. She is really accommodating and responsive. homeworks are pretty easy but if you have trouble office hours are great. If you have any questions she is very approachable. If you put in the effort you'll do great.

The course contents are extremely unorganized and everywhere. Not only the students are not sure what is going on, the TAs are also confused by her specs. Her rubric for assignment makes no sense, you have to spend 80% time on figuring out what she wants and only 20% time on ACTUAL learning. NEVER take her course.

Extremely rude and disrespectful as a professor. Non-inclusive and non empathetic towards students. I had a terrible experience with her for auditing her class. She lacks patience and basic decency to talk to students.

grading is point based so you just work up to the grade you want and van lent gave us extra assignments to get more points. GSIs have a ton of office hours to help with homework/projects. programming exams are almost all questions taken from the homework

I enjoyed this class a lot as I learned how to code without any experience before! There are a lot of assignments and exams as the class is structured in a points system. There are many office hours offered and the GSIs are very helpful. The only thing is that it is a little difficult to learn the concepts without a textbook or direct instruction.

I found professor Van Lent extremely caring for her students. Attendance wasn't mandatory, but I went to every lecture because she was very caring towards class. Yes, there are minor inconveniences with issues for the auto-grader, but she accommodates extra points for that reason. She's really funny and tells you what to expect for exams.
Very bad experience, very unclear guidance

I LOVE Colleen. She knows her stuff & is great at teaching it. She's such a caring professor & I learned so much about web development and accessibility from her (especially with her personal stories). When students didn't understand a concept she was never frustrated, & was willing to sit with you for however long it took to get it.

As a non-SI major, I really appreciated how inspiring and caring Dr. Van Lent is. As a professor, she is incredibly kind and one of the best human beings I've met. She shares her passion for teaching with the students, and uses great analogies to connect with students. Exams can be tough tho.
Very casual and lose work style - always updating the homework requirements one day before it's due, which is very disturbing and annoying.

I respectfully disagree with the previous rating; I'm taking the same class (which I think the previous commenter mistyped) and Prof. Van Lent has extremely available, accommodating, and approachable. The autograder issue occurs when code doesn't meet the specificationI haven't had an issue with it. Others who've had get direct help with it.

This class is so stupid, the auto grader is so stupid. There are so many ways to write the CSS to get the same effect but the professor and GSIs just choose to use the auto grader which is so rigid and it doesn't make sense at all! Why can't you just grade people's homework by LOOKING INTO THEIR CODE?!

Colleen was probably one of the best instructors I've taken while at U-M. She's always willing to answer any questions, and her teaching style were lecture with in-class activities, which really helps in understand the material. After COVID closed our school, she was still able to maintain the same level of lecture through digital means.

Colleen is an incredibly caring professor and will really help you learn web development in the course. She's really understanding when you have difficulties and plans the course around helping people with the problems they have. Highly recommend

SI 339 was an excellent course. While attendance is not mandatory, her lectures are very clear and sometimes she will even do the homework in front of you. There are a lot of assignments, but none of them are unreasonable and there is a lot of extra credit if you miss something. Very good course for learning web development.

One of the best professors I have ever had. You don't HAVE to go to class but you will want to! I learned so much from the examples and practice we did even though I came in with no web development experience. She really cares about students and the people we design for. If you have the chance, take a class with her!

Colleen is one of the nicest professors I've ever had. She cares about her students and wants the entire class to learn and do well. SI 206 was restructured this semester for the better, but I actually wish we had moved faster and learned even more. Overall, it was a great course and I enjoyed the class a lot!

She's sweet, caring and honest. While fairly decent in large lectures, she's genuinely amazing one-on-one. This semester, SI206 changed a lot from prior ones, so there were still a few kinks in course structure and syllabus. However, I'm sure it'll get better in subsequent terms. Tl;dr: Colleen's great; be sure to actually speak and work with her.
Great all around teacher. Could have a little more work that pays off, but kills it. So caring and nice.

Often has errors in his powerpoint presentations and gets confused and flustered when the class asks questions about the errors. Doesn't keep office hours. Obviously hates students, so why is he a teacher?

Assignments are VERY time-consuming, and late work receives no credit. Attendance is graded through class questions, and incorrect answers result in no attendance points. Extra credit was mentioned, but very limited. Lectures move quickly and can be unclear. Exams are difficult and not well aligned with practice materials. Take only if required.
Unfair. No late submissions for partial credit, long homeworks, boring lectures. Material is not taught well, exams hard even after completing practice exams. Do not take if not required.

Removed the weekly quizzes from the W24 semester. Mandatory i-clicker questions that are fairly easy. Romero sets reasonable expectations and deadlines and is an above-average lecturer. Strong at linking arbitrary graph-theory principles to real-world examples.

Hardest SI class, old reviews are outdated. The class has been changed to have an insane workload, weekly quizes. Charles and Aparna are incredible its a shame they dont know whats going on in the class as well sometimes. Not of their fault , just a class thats hard to be hard, make friends
Insane amount of homework, least favorite class
Prof Daniel Romero is very kind and a good advisor on coursework

Make sure to get people to check homework in. Can easily get a 0 on a section by getting the wrong solution, even with the right process. It was the most difficult and most rewarding course. Professor Romero is smart and extremely knowledgeable about the field. He has normal English abilities, idk what other people are talking about. Fantastic guy

Daniel really knows his stuff (and his English, unlike what some other students are saying). The class is very well organized and flows smoothly. Average amount of work for a 4 credit class. HW assignments weekly, half are with partners. There are iClickers each class, but there is a grading option where you don't need to attend. He is very fair.

Online textbook is free but very complex. Lecture slides are posted but not the most helpful (+ his English is 7/10). Iclickers can be done remotely so you dont *have* to go to class but I recommend it because he clarifies vague slides Likes to play Jazz music during iclickers 2 Exams: Fair. Homeworks can be really tough/confusing- make friends.

Had him for both 301 and 370. Very solid 370 teacher and really thought I got a solid grasp of data science fundamentals thru him. Kind of threw a lot at us and would've appreciated fewer topics and diving deeper into them. Regardless, I'm motivated to dive deeper into data science bc of this class and highly recommend it!

Pay attention in lecture and you are guaranteed to do well. A bulk of your grade is take home assignments that are easy to do well in if you put in the effort and it helps you gauge your understanding. Tests are difficult if you fall behind but staying on top of assignments will prevent that.

Show up to class for iclickers. The exams are a breeze, Romero is a nice guy and his lecture slide content is pretty reflective of the exams. Would definitely recommend as a flex tech to any CS majors

Professor Romero includes a lot of information on his lecture slides, which I appreciated. He is a nice and competent professor. This class is not incredibly difficult, but given the many concepts and theories we learned, it requires time and effort. I enjoyed the class overall.

Romero is decent. He can be nice, funny, modest, and generally helpful. The class isn't too mentally taxing, it simply requires adequate time in order to get a good grade. No need to avoid him or this class, but there isn't anything really spectacular here.

Most boring lectures ever. He simply reads off the slides and makes attendance mandatory. Half the time he is wrong and can't answer questions you have. This is a completely worthless class taught by a completely incompetent professor. He also gives a very long assignment every single week that is simply busy work. Avoid this if possible.
Romero is a good professor. Not the greatest professor I've ever had, but by no means bad. Certainly not someone you need to avoid. The class is average difficulty, it's not hard to get a B.

Ton of homework which got harder bc the class basically failed the 1st exam, did NOT prepare us well for 1st exam, 2nd one was much better, has a group project done thru the term, lenient grading on that, gives great lectures, can speak well on content, did not understand failure to prepare students for exam 1 based off incomparable practice exam

Ton of homework which got harder bc the class basically failed the 1st exam, did NOT prepare us well for 1st exam, 2nd one was much better, a group project done thru the term, lenient grading on that, gives great lectures, can speak well on content, did not understand failure to prepare students for exam 1 based off incomparable practice exam
Poor ability to convey lecture content. Disorganized slides and lack of clarity on what to expect on exam. Worst
Terrible experience, wouldn't recommend. Much more challenging than the course requirements make it seem. Expect to put a lot of hours to this class throughout the semester.

Jurgens did really well with lectures and engagement, but there were some issues with communication throughout the term here and there; otherwise, super well-structured course with lots of relevant and rigorous content. Open-ended final project to let you get creative. 4 homeworks, 1 final. Unfortunately had to drop, but really liked what I saw

Jurgens can be extremely accommodating when approached with extension requests or questions. The lectures were great, but the reworked class was highly disorganized in structure. Assignments were ludicrously challenging at first due to administrative issues. I anticipate 650 will be much better next fall since all these issues were identified.

SI 650 has been the most time consuming class I've ever taken, both at the undergraduate and graduate levels. Moreover, the assignments were poorly designed and frequently contained errors. Don't take this class if you value your time. Expect to be frustrated, this was a bad experience for me.

It was clear that Jurgens had contempt for the class, even explicitly making remarks to that effect when pressed. The homeworks were the worst-planned homeworks I've ever had, and betrayed an utter lack of preparation on the part of the instructors, and overall lack of care on their part for the experience of the students.

This is one of the worst professors I've ever had. He knows the content but is a bad lecturer and doesn't care about the students AT ALL. There were multiple complains from the students about the difficulty of the course and the professor refused to modify the structure of the course or provide help/resources. HARD/LONG homeworks. AVOID HIM.

Curriculum changes this year. NEVER TAKE his course pls. This is my sincere advice. Everything in this course is terrible. You will never know this unless you've experienced. This is my last year as a student, in the first half of my life, I've never seen such a course. Probably in my rest of life --- I wouldn't suffer as what now I am suffering.
He was so awesome

Horrible horrible.... He seems to completely ignore the continuous influx of negative feedbacks from students on his awfully designed HW (EXTREME WORKLOAD) and acting if those are only coming from a minority of the class (so no need to deal with and improve)? SI department should be ashamed for letting someone like him to teach the core class.

Dr. Jurgens is an awful professor who has no concept of his own deficiencies. Despite MULTIPLE students identifying major issues with the class (e.g., huge errors in instructions, broken AGs, etc.), he refused repeatedly to improve the class. He has no idea how long his assignments take and spends lectures waxing poetically on nothing. DO NOT TAKE
extremely HEAVY workload with no detailed instruction. His guide is a mess and help nothing but make you completely lost. The lecture tells nothing because he just assumed you know everything :)

Don't take this professor! Avoid taking his courses as much as you can. He doesn't care about student needs or effective teaching. He isn't flexible, so if you don't learn through his teaching style or at his pace you should get a tutor ASAP. I think he lacks self-awareness because he's offensive to students and non-empathic. GSI's won't help you.

Prof Jurgens is a visionary and great teacher. He is very intelligent and good with communication. The projects/ HW he gave us helped find me job in difficult market. He was also one of the first person to tell us importance of LLM models and encouraged to try out models like GPT (chatgpt) , chatbot etc.

He was one of the worst professors I have taken at the University of Michigan. From the start, he never seemed fully prepared for lecture, and was rather unaware of the content in the course (even assignments that had already been assigned to us). There was unclear expectations throughout the course, with students unsure of how things are graded.

Was so unprepared for lectures, seemed like he had no idea what he was doing. Gave us 10 minutes for quizzes that should have been 20 minutes at least. I had a concussion the day of a quiz and he told me I would just have to use it as one of my two dropped quizzes and could not make it up. Do not take this class if he is teaching it.

SI's David Jurgens is currently a highlight of my semester for being approachable, compassionate, attentive to student inquiries during lecture, and great at explaining things in a class that kinda intimidates me sometimes. Also, with the quarantine beard, I'm getting some Deorio vibes.
Great lecturer
The WORST Teacher Ever

Don't recommend! He is really bad at explaining concepts and being impatient to students. I am confused about 95% of the course and all my classmates said so. When you put lots of effort in writing proposals or literature reviews, he won't provide any feedback too.

Worst class ever. Workload is unreasonably heavy and the assignments are not practical and no help for student in real work. The professor didn't reply students email and not aware students difficulty in learning. I even need to pay to attend an expo to get 5% of grade.
Tough tests and assignments. Heavy workload.
He is a great teacher. Beware of his examinations though!

SI110- His lectures got long and covered a lot of material. You don't need to go to lecture though, but do the readings because you'll need those in reading and event reports as sources. Insane amount of writing for this class. Don't take if not planning on majoring/ minoring in SI, take an easier social science instead.

Best lecturer I've ever had. If you're interested in ethics in information, data use, privacy, surveillance, etc, take his Information Ethics course. His workload is challenging and time-intensive, but his lectures are fantastic and he will gladly help if you're having trouble. You'll learn a ton in this class, it's worth it.

Lectures are just the examples from the book, but Dr. O'Brien explains the material in a far more accessible manner. The tests were difficult-- study hard. Go to office hours and make sure you know what will be on the test.

Lectures are insanely good and Elle answers questions very well and clearly. She's an asset to UMSI. My GSI for the course graded very leniently. Teaching team was very accommodating with deadlines and such. Ask questions about more advanced material while you have Elle as a professor, because she knows her stats and can explain things so clearly.

Love this sweet old man. 441 is an unique, one of a kind tech business course here. You can literally build anything not just limited mobile app here, as long as you find it commercially promising. Don't blame him for not coding. in other eecs courses those professors also just gave concepts and don't teaching programming details.

Irrelevant to the difficulty of EECS 441 (some people complain that it's too easy and we don't learn that much), this professor is really awesome. He was the most enthusiastic professor I've ever seen in U-M, and brought life to my dull online-only classes during the pandemic. Definitely take this professor's class if you have the opportunity!
This man is the funniest professor I've had at Michigan, he's super fun and very easygoing. Just do what he says and make him happy, he'll help you out where you need it.

Although his course is not easy for a typical computer science student, I learned a lot in his course. Unlike other computer science courses, he taught not technical concepts, but ideas and future about computer science and high technology. He helped me to find a possibility of the larger business world.
Very enthusiastic. Would like to take his course again.
He really knows what he is teaching, very hilarious and inspiring!
Very inspirational
A great professor! I learned a lot in this course!
Basically the easiest an MDE class could possibly get. Not a ton of work compared to your other options. You don't really learn much, but hey I'll take the good grade, that's for sure.

Soloway is a character, plain and simple. He's a man with big ideas and amazing comedic talent, and he absolutely sucks at computer science. He couldn't program his way out of a paper bag, but he'll tell you why your mobile app is trash, and he'll make it funny. He also doesn't usually fail people. If you try and fail, you still pass.
This professor is mean.

I wasn't particularly interested in the discussion of current events that Soloway likes, but by the end of the class I was glad I had been exposed to his views and to the industry side of programming. He has quite a personality, and he's pretty funny. If you have something you want to learn, this is the class to incorporate it into a project.
Would often ask the GSIs/IAs for help on solving problem. As a newbie to C++, it was very frustrating and confusing - if my prof can't write programs, why should I learn?
He's a decent guy, but a horrible professor.
Teaches C++, does not know C++. He is constantly corrected by his students. Take this class with Dorf, she is amazing and knows her stuff.

I found Prof Soloway very inspirational. For those taking his "Smartphone Programming" course, beware - he does not know how to program in any language and counts on you to teach yourself how to do so. He really just oversees projects, albeit very loosely and talks a lot of tech news (which I don't mind because I love tech news).

He loves the current events, which is cool, interesting, and helps students learn about the real world, but the topics and time (up to 30+ minutes) he invests in them isn't appropriate for some of the courses he teaches, such as in EECS183 or EECS498 (too much non-mobile related news). Makes sense in EECS497. Also, he doesn't know how to program.

The 183 commentors were right in that he spends a lot of time on current events, but it makes sense in 498, which is just a senior projects class. He gives you a lot of freedom and not a lot of oversight, so be aware of that. He's rather absent-minded, but the things you will learn about the practicality of the real world will be a good balance.

Whenever you have other choices, don't choose his class. He talks too much news during the first 10-30 mins in his lectures. He never posts things on Ctools or reply emails. Really disappointed and right after mid1 I started to take another professor ML Dorf's class which you can expect to learn something .

Professor Soloway is not a good professor. He often gives wrong answers/explanations during lecture, only to be corrected by the students. He says weird things during class, such as "WHAMO!" and "AHA" in a high pitched, awkward voice. Half the class is wasted on current events. If you're looking for an education, take Dorf's class.

He has a humungous ego making him think he is the funniest guy in the best field in the world emphasizing money money money blah blah money this greed that - he's too busy making annoying jokes and side comments that he never finishes the lecture let alone starts it - I find myself turning to the dorfinator's lecture since he is an idiot
He's funny and really cares that you understand.

Best professor I've ever had - you need to go to class, pref one of his smaller ones as a lot of it is self-taught. I also don't know anyone who has actually failed (or gotten below an A-) in any of his classes that did the work.
He will let you do your pet project, and let you have freedom and fun. The problem is that you will learn very little from him.

I assume we're using the term "professor" loosely here... I really don't remember learning much of anything in this class except to make sure you work with friends so that they don't ditch you on coding the project... even when my group confessed they hadn't helped at all, Soloway still gave me a C for not finishing every last feature he wanted.

Is good for creative people who don't like to satisfy autograder. Lets you work on your own interesting project! Although his "lectures" are a bit useless since he just has free-form discussion, but projects make the class worth taking.
Great Professor. Skills I learned in his class have extremely valuable in the work force.
Loud almost nasal voice and rubs his stomach a lot. Other than that, fantastic professor. Runs more of a discussion than teach. His explanations are fairly well thought out.

This is my second class with Erin and she's a gem. Arguably the most caring professor who I've ever had. The readings (posted on Canvas, no textbook) can be challenging, but she does a great job at breaking it down. Decent amount of extra credit opportunity. The one minus is that she can take awhile to grade.

EASY class. Legit show up to class and discussion, do the couple writing assignments, and you will get B+ at the VERY least, you will almost certainly get an A- as long as you can write decently. NO Exams. Open note quizzes where you work with other kids. Legit, I'm begging you, easy class if you need social science distributions. Krupkachiller

Her lectures are okay, but most of the lecture content is just a summary of the reading for that day. The quizzes in class are very specific, which make sense why she made them open-book quizzes. But still, pay attention in class if you want to get an A.

She seems knowledgeable on the topics, but frankly she just regurgitates the readings during lectures. The instruction for our semester was really disorganized. You don't really have to do the readings if you're paying attention to what she focuses on in class; quizzes have weirdly specific questions about the readings. Put effort into projects.

Great lecturer and interesting class. There is a lot more work than expected, get ready to do a lot of reading and writing but pretty easy A. She was really thoughtful about the fact that some of the projects take a while and how students might be busy at different points of the semester so she made a lot of extensions and open note quizzes.

Erin rocks. Not easy-breezy, but not nearly as difficult as some people make her classes out to be. I realized that the people complaining weren't used to academic reading and writing. If you're good at reading and writing, you'll do just fine. I found her theories of social influence class to be extremely useful -- grounded in rigorous studies.

Erin is a consummate professional. She's highly-skilled at teaching and research (arguably the best professor at SI). It's clear that she thoughtfully prepares for lectures, breaking down somewhat difficult, but very useful, material into digestible pieces. Erin may be the most down-to-earth, empathic, and simply cool, type-A person I've met.

Erin is an awesome teacher but she is a very tough grader. She has a great style but will assign very tough and a lot of readings. Don't get placated by those extra credits. They are still not enough to pass the class easily.
Professor irresponsive and disrespect to students.

The courses approach to information visualization gives little practical knowledge about the field and focuses very little on actually creating visualizations that are not your standard bar graph. Teaching assistants were very little help and professor a bit unapproachable.

Workload and time required for this course is unrealistic, especially during pandemic. The requirements for homework can be vague, confusing, and unclear, with confusing and conflicting information. The coding homework assignments had many bugs discovered by students, which indicates to me that the teaching team did not test the projects.

Eytan is humorous and all but his course is very much oriented towards human computer interaction like every thing in school of information. His instructions are not always clear but his grading is really tough.
Eytan is really nice and humor. The course is interesting and useful, with appropriate workload. Grading is fair with detailed comments on your assignments.

This professor is so unprepared for class, there was never a syllabus. Assignments were poorly described, his expectations were not clear but his grading was tough. He communicated via email posts that were short and unhelpful. He was extremely slow in grading and retuning assignments.

I was worried that a three hour lecture would feel boring and long, but Professor Sparr's lectures were always super engaging, and felt more like a conversation rather than being lectured at. The readings were also always relevant and interesting, and never felt like busy work. Loved this class!!

I took every class with Faith that I could, being a comm major interested in law school. Learned a whole bunch that will surely be useful in the future!! Faith likes to lecture a lot, but she knows what shes talking about and is very respectable. Go to office hours and she is always willing to help. Participate in class to get the most out of it!

This was my favorite class I have ever taken at U of M. Professor Sparr is very smart and is good at presenting different arguments and making students think. I would recommend her to everyone. As long as you read the cases and come to class for discussion on them, you will do great on the tests. The tests are very straight forward. Take this class

Basically you get assigned a case which you read and then present to the class about twice a term! If you don't understand anything, Faith is very knowledgable and open to questions and discussion. A very interesting class for anyone interested in law with a huge emphasis on the First Amendment. Would recommend her for sure!
Very very boring class. She doesn't teach anything - makes the students present the cases. If you are used to engaging/interesting classes, avoid this one.

Faith is one of my favorite professors at Michigan. She's a lawyer, so it only makes sense to take a law-oriented class from someone who's had the firsthand experience. She's brilliant - she knows the material, makes it easy to understand even if you're not interested in law, and has very realistic expectations. Fair, fun, highly recommend.

I've taken all three of Professor Sparr's classes and she is by far my favorite professor at Michigan. Yeah there is a lot of material on the exams, but if you study then you'll get an A and there is not busy work or papers. She's super helpful and class discussions are really interesting.

I'd never taken a law-related class before, but I found it really interesting and not too difficult. Faith really clarifies things in discussion, and she welcomes differing opinions. She's great. The workload is reasonable, mostly just daily readings and then two exams.

Since I wasn't familiar with law to begin with the material was challenging BUT she is a great teacher and the workload for the course is very reasonable. Tests are daunting because they cover a lot but she is a fair grader and even if you don't know much about law you can do well if you work hard, actually do the readings and attend class
Great Professor

I had no prior interest in taking Media Law but I needed Comm credits. Ended up loving the class. Professor Sparr is really knowledgeable in this field. It's definitely not media law (its First Amendment law, actually) but the material is relevant. Not an easy class but if you're willing to read/study you'll do fine. 3 exams for your grade

I don't know why this class was called media law, because it had nothing to do with the media or comm at all. This class was incredibly boring and the exams were graded by a GSI no one knew and ridiculously hard. This was probably one of the worst classes I've taken while at the university, avoid.

This definitely is a challenging class, but Sparr is willing to help you and help you do well. YOU HAVE TO DO THE READINGS AND GO TO CLASS IN ORDER TO DO WELL. If you do the work and study hard, you will do well. Sparr is really nice, funny and intelligent.

Taken both 452 & 459 and by FAR my absolute favorite professor on campus!!! The readings arent that difficult at all, theyre just cases that you go over in class (so make sure to go). Exams are blue book exams, as long as you know your stuff youre good, she definitely doesnt try to trick you and is really fair on her grading. I love her!!

She is smart, but the readings are really dense since you are reading law books. She is a nice person but since all of the work in the class is done by case briefs via students, if you do not understand something she does not really clarify it. if you want to work hard, take this class, but if you're not interested in the subject, stay away

GREAT professor! She is engaging, funny, witty and really knows her stuff! She is one of the most intelligent yet approachable professors I've had at UM and I would surely reccomend her classes (I took 459, too). If you have perfect attendance(2 ex absences) she will give u 5 extra credit points, so go and admire her style and grace!! REALLY COOL!

3 bluebook exams on class discussed material, present a case brief, and attend class (she's a hardass on this). While a relatively simple class structure-wise, this was one of the most dull courses I have ever taken at U of M. I will never take another course with this teacher.

The 3 exams are very similar and you will succeed if you ask questions and prepare for the exams (no surprises). Each student is responsible for presenting a case from the textbook about twice throughout the semester, but other than that the work load is very light. Everyone posts notes on CTools prior to the exams which aids in studying.

She is one of the smartest professors i have ever had in four years. I had never taken a comm class and took it because it sounded mildly interesting. class is not the easiest thing but there are only 3 exams, if you know the material, memorize the important stuff, you will do fine. She is a great teacher.
Professor Sparr is to the point, clear and concise. She will help you if you ask and her exams are straightforward. If you go to class, memorize and study you will get an A.

Interesting class, though the title is sort of misleading because a lot of the time the class is not about cyber law. F Sparr is a tough test grader but at the same time reasonable. She is a lawyer and pretty smart too. She is good looking! Definitely take if you're a comm major or are pre-law, even if you're not interested in cyber law.

Lectures were too discussion based and not enough of her insight, which limited our ability to prep for exams. Although she's a lawyer, she's not necessarily the best teacher. Expect her to to be a tough grade.
No Comments

Professor Sparr is a great addition to the Comm. studies department. She is very knowledgeable, as she is a certified attorney, and very helpful as well. Her exams are straightforward, but the papers tend to be somewhat difficult as she is a strict grade. Overall, great prof.
